title: Make your edge persistent: the two-page voice method
text:
The morning’s two-pass demo showed the whole thesis in five minutes: ask AI naively and you get the same slick, generic answer everyone else gets. Add what only you hold – your context, your taste, your angle – and the output gets a soul. That’s the edge, injected one prompt at a time.
This page is how you make that edge permanent, so you don’t have to re-supply it on every request. The technique comes from AI researcher Ethan Mollick; the tool below is free and runs in your browser.



Give it a sample of your writing. A few hundred words of something you actually wrote – an email, a report, a blog post. The more it sounds like you, the better.
Let it write a style brief. Ask the AI to analyse the sample and produce a one-to-two-page description of your style, including instructions for how to write in it. (This is the key move: you want a description of your voice, not more of your writing.)
Paste that brief into your AI’s custom instructions. Tell it “write in this style” for future tasks. From then on, the generic average tilts toward your average.




Style Mirror (stylemirror.eduserver.au) does steps 1 and 2 for you, in the browser:

Open the Analyze tab, paste your writing, hit Extract Style DNA.
It returns an editable Style DNA profile – tone, structure, vocabulary, quirks – which is your style brief.
Copy that profile into your AI platform’s custom instructions.

It’s provider-agnostic. It runs on a local model (Ollama), which means your writing sample never leaves your machine – worth choosing if the sample is work-sensitive. (That’s the data-responsibility call this course keeps returning to.)



Mollick is blunt that the result is a “slight parody” of you, not a perfect clone. Don’t expect it to carry a long-form piece like a book. That caveat is the interesting bit, because it’s the thesis landing a second time:

Even personalised, the AI is still producing an average of you – a smoothed, stylised version of your voice. The part it can’t capture is your live judgement: the taste call you make in the moment, the thing you’d say here but not there.

So the goal isn’t to outsource your voice. It’s to clear the generic baseline out of the way – the part anyone could have written – so the bit that’s left, the part only you would write, is easier to spot and protect.
That’s also why mimicking yourself beats asking the AI to mimic a famous writer. Hemingway’s style is already in the training data – it’s the ultimate generic average. Your voice is the one thing only you can supply.



A two-page style brief, pasted into your AI’s custom instructions, that quietly turns “generic” into “yours” – and a clearer sense of where the edge still lives after that: with you.

title: Scale / Pivot / Kill
text:
When you’re running a pilot or testing a new idea, eventually you hit a moment of truth: Do you expand it, change it, or stop it?
This framework helps you make that decision based on data, not emotion.

title: Scale / Pivot / Kill
text:
When you’re running a pilot or testing a new idea, eventually you hit a moment of truth: Do you expand it, change it, or stop it?
This framework helps you make that decision based on data, not emotion.

title: Scale / Pivot / Kill
text:
The Three Paths

SCALE ✓
What it means: Roll out the project to full scope. It’s working. Go big.
When to choose: - You’re hitting most of your success criteria - Gaps are minor and don’t undermine the core value - Team is engaged and ready - You have confidence this works at scale
Example: “Accuracy hit 91%, satisfaction is 83%, team adoption is 80%. The small gaps are worth the value we’re delivering. Scale it.”



PIVOT 🔄
What it means: Keep going, but change direction. Refine the scope, fix gaps, or adjust the approach.
When to choose: - Core value is proven, but something needs improvement - Gaps are solvable (not fundamental failures) - You need more data before scaling - Time and resources are available
Example: “Accuracy is 88% vs. 90% target. That’s solvable: 4 more weeks of model retraining will get us there. PIVOT: Extend 4 weeks, then reassess for scale.”



KILL ✗
What it means: Stop the project. The approach isn’t working. Move on.
When to choose: - The core idea doesn’t work (not fixable gaps) - You’ve proven the assumption wrong - The cost to continue exceeds the value - Better alternatives exist
Example: “Customers rejected the approach. We built for speed but they want personalisation. A different approach is needed. KILL this version and start over.”

title: Scale / Pivot / Kill
text:
How to Decide: The 3-Step Process

Step 1: Score Your Criteria (5 minutes)
Look at your Go/No-Go criteria from planning. For each one, did you hit the target?



Criteria
Target
Actual
Hit?




Accuracy
≥90%
88%
❌


Satisfaction
≥80%
82%
✓


Response Time
<4 hrs
3.5 hrs
✓


Cost per Query
$12
$14
❌


Team Adoption
80%
75%
⚠️


Escalations
<2%
3.5%
❌



Count the checkmarks. If you hit 4+ of 6, you’re in SCALE/PIVOT territory. If you hit 2-3, you’re in PIVOT territory. If you hit <2, you’re in KILL territory.



Step 2: Solvable vs. Fundamental (5 minutes)
Look at the gaps. Are they solvable (need more time/resources) or fundamental (the approach doesn’t work)?
Solvable gaps: - “Accuracy is 88% vs. 90%, that’s a 2-point gap we can close with more training” - “Team adoption is low; they just need more exposure” - “Cost is higher than expected; we can optimise once we hit scale”
Fundamental gaps: - “Customers hate this approach” - “The core technology doesn’t work for this use case” - “We can’t get the data we need”
If all gaps are solvable: You can SCALE or PIVOT. If some gaps are fundamental: You PIVOT or KILL.



Step 3: Make Your Call (5 minutes)
Based on steps 1-2, decide:

SCALE if: Most criteria met + no fundamental gaps + team ready
PIVOT if: Some criteria met + gaps are solvable + you have time
KILL if: Few criteria met + fundamental gaps + better alternatives exist

title: Scale / Pivot / Kill
text:
Common Mistakes to Avoid
❌ Sunk Cost Fallacy “We spent $150K, so we have to scale it.” Reality: That money is gone. Only future value matters.
❌ Cherry-Picking Data “Customers are happy, so ignore the accuracy gap.” Reality: All criteria matter. You can’t ignore metrics that fail.
❌ **Confusing “Not Perfect” with “Broken” ”We’re 2 points away from target, so we must kill it.”* Reality: Small gaps = PIVOT, not KILL.
❌ Making Emotional Decisions “I feel like this could work…” Reality: Use your criteria. Data beats gut feel.

title: Scale / Pivot / Kill
text:
The Decision Template
Use this to document your call:
Decision: [SCALE / PIVOT / KILL]
Criteria Met: [List how many of your success criteria you hit]
Why? [2-3 sentences explaining your logic]
Trade-offs: [What are you giving up?]
Next Steps: [If SCALE: rollout plan. If PIVOT: what changes? If KILL: what’s next?]

title: Scale / Pivot / Kill
text:
Remember

Your Go/No-Go criteria from the planning phase are your anchor
Data beats emotion
“Not perfect yet” is not the same as “doesn’t work”
You can decide with imperfect information
Most projects PIVOT; that’s normal and healthy

The goal isn’t to make the perfect decision. It’s to make the best decision you can with the data you have.

title: Data governance: proportionate, not prohibitive
text:
Many organisations have landed on blanket prohibitions: “do not upload company documents to AI.” These policies treat all AI interaction as equally risky, and they conflate several distinct concerns – some legitimate, some based on fundamental misunderstandings of how large language models work.


The rational core of caution is about the pipeline before training: the transmission, processing, and storage of data, not what happens inside the model.

Data residency and sovereignty. Regulations (GDPR, the Australian Privacy Act, sector-specific rules) require certain data to stay within specific jurisdictions. Pasting a document into a consumer AI tool transmits it to external infrastructure, often in another country. The act of transmission itself may be the compliance violation.
Contractual obligations. NDAs, client agreements, and government contracts define which systems may process covered data. Sending it to a third-party AI service may breach those terms – even if the provider handles it perfectly.
Logging and retention. AI providers may retain prompts for safety monitoring or debugging. A sensitive document sits on someone else’s servers for a retention period. Enterprise agreements negotiate specific terms around this.
Supply chain risk. AI providers, like any SaaS vendor, can be breached. This isn’t the model leaking data; it’s the company’s servers being compromised. Standard infosec, managed through vendor risk assessments.




Problems emerge when organisations conflate those legitimate infrastructure concerns with fears about model behaviour – specifically, the belief that someone could extract uploaded documents from the model itself.
This misunderstands how LLMs work. LLMs interpolate; they do not retrieve. If data is used for training at all (enterprise tiers typically exclude it), it becomes a vanishingly small statistical signal distributed across billions of parameters. It is not stored as a retrievable file. It is dissolved into the model’s general capability like a drop of ink in a swimming pool. There is no mechanism by which another user could query the model and reconstruct your document, because the model never stored it as a document.



Many organisations enforcing strict AI prohibitions happily allow employees to paste sensitive content into email, Slack, shared drives, and dozens of other SaaS tools – all of which carry the exact same transmission, storage, and jurisdictional risks. AI feels riskier because it’s newer, not because the risk profile is different.




Classify the data. Public, internal, confidential, regulated?
Match the tool to the classification. Enterprise AI with data-processing agreements for internal/confidential work; consumer tools for public and non-sensitive brainstorming; regulated data needs a specific assessment.
Manage the real risks. Never paste credentials, API keys, or access tokens. De-identify personal information. Understand the provider’s logging and retention terms.
Drop the fictional risks. Stop treating model extraction as a plausible threat. Focus governance effort where the actual exposure is: transmission, storage, jurisdiction, and contractual compliance.

The goal is professional data hygiene applied consistently across all tools – not AI exceptionalism driven by misunderstanding.
Adapted from the AI Skills Passport’s “Why Enterprise AI Data Governance Gets It Wrong.”

title: Frameworks overview
text:
These are durable, tool-agnostic frameworks: they work regardless of which AI model you’re using or which one comes out next quarter. They span both halves of the day – the task scale (morning) and the project scale (afternoon).



Average / Precise × Small / Large. Two questions turn “where does a human stay in the loop?” from a gut feel into a decision you can defend. Learned in the morning at the task level, reused in the afternoon at the project level.



Role · Task · Context · Format. Context is where your edge enters: the specifics only you hold.



“Done” can’t be specified · the demo is a trap · the data is the uncertainty · verification is the product · generic competence is the baseline.



At each gate, the honest question isn’t “is it perfect?” It’s whether the evidence justifies continuing. Killing early is success.

Drawn from the free companion book, Conversation, Not Delegation. Go there to dig deeper on any of these.

title: Sprint 2: Stakeholders & human-in-the-loop
text:
Your initiative: _______________________ Group: __________
A working AI system isn’t a tool. It’s a division of labour between human judgement and machine output. This sprint you design that division, and the stakeholder plan around it. Interview Emma (Managing Director), Tom (Customer Service) and David (CFO).

title: Sprint 2: Stakeholders & human-in-the-loop
text:
1. Stakeholder plan









Stakeholder
What they need / fear
How you’ll manage them
Power × Interest




Emma (Managing Director / sponsor)


High / High


David (CFO)





Tom (CS / frontline)

title: Sprint 2: Stakeholders & human-in-the-loop
text:
2. Human-in-the-loop checkpoints
For every point where AI output could be wrong and it matters, design a checkpoint.








Checkpoint (where a human checks AI output)
Who has the expertise to actually evaluate it?
Does this checkpoint build that expertise, or just consume it?





















The evaluator’s advantage: experienced people spot the confident-but-wrong answer because they know what right looks like. Judgement is unevenly distributed, so staff your checkpoints with the right humans, not just any human.
The apprenticeship trap: if you automate all the grunt work, you stop producing the next generation of evaluators. Mark at least one checkpoint that you keep human deliberately, to build capability, not only for quality.

title: Sprint 2: Stakeholders & human-in-the-loop
section: 3. Sprint close — defend the one you won’t automate
text:
3. Sprint close — defend the one you won’t automate
This is how you know the sprint is done. Pick the single human-in-the-loop checkpoint you are most confident is non-negotiable, then defend it:

State it — which checkpoint, and who has the expertise to actually evaluate it.
Name the evidence — why this one? What breaks if a human isn’t here? Pass the builds-vs-consumes test: does keeping it human build the next evaluator’s judgement, or only consume the one you have?
Take one challenge. A group (or your facilitator) asks: “Could you automate this and still build the capability you’ll need next year?”


If you can’t answer the challenge, your checkpoint design isn’t finished — go back to section 2.


Leave Sprint 2 with: a stakeholder plan and a human-in-the-loop checkpoint design where each checkpoint names its evaluator and is marked builds-vs-consumes.

title: Inventory optimisation
text: Your group is the delivery lead for this funded initiative.

title: Inventory optimisation
text:
The mandate
The board has approved $1.1M (the largest of the four) to build AI inventory optimisation: forecasting demand and automating replenishment across 50 stores plus the online channel, to cut both stockouts and overstock.
What the board thinks it bought: “major efficiency gains across the whole network.”
Your job: deliver it without the predictable failures, and define a “good enough” forecast that’s safe to act on automatically.

title: Inventory optimisation
section: Why this one is hard to deliver
text:
Why this one is hard to deliver

It is the broadest in scope (every store, every category), so the temptation to boil the ocean is strong, and the blast radius of a bad forecast is large.
It runs straight into legacy systems: the POS and inventory systems are 10–15 years old, and online/store data is siloed. Integration is where this project lives or dies.
A forecast that’s “about right” in aggregate (the demo) can be badly wrong for a specific store/category, and that’s where the cost and the staff frustration show up.

title: Inventory optimisation
text:
The data reality (ask Priya)
Priya’s honest read: “significant value potential, but data quality issues to resolve first; integration complexity is underestimated. 9–12 months. Start with ONE category pilot, prove value, then scale.” Her advice basically pre-writes your roadmap, if you ask her.

title: Inventory optimisation
section: Who to interview, and the tension they bring
text:
Who to interview, and the tension they bring

Priya Sharma (Data): pushes the single-category pilot; data-quality realism.
Marcus Kim (CIO): knows the legacy-systems pain better than anyone; also impatient for transformation.
Sarah Thompson (COO): operational reality across stores; the people who’ll act on (or ignore) the forecasts. She slows things down, sometimes rightly.

title: Inventory optimisation
text:
Your starting question for “good enough”
What forecast accuracy, in which category, justifies automating replenishment versus keeping it as a recommendation a store manager approves? How do you scope a pilot narrow enough to prove value but real enough to matter?

title: Dynamic pricing
text: Your group is the delivery lead for this funded initiative.

title: Dynamic pricing
text:
The mandate
The board has approved $850K to build AI-driven dynamic pricing, automatically adjusting prices across fashion and homewares lines in response to demand, competitor moves, inventory levels and margin targets. The case that won funding: a “significant revenue opportunity” and competitors “already doing it.”
What the board thinks it bought: “smarter prices that lift margin, live this year.”
Your job: deliver it without the predictable failures, and define what “good enough” pricing decisions look like before any price goes live automatically.

title: Dynamic pricing
section: Why this one is hard to deliver
text:
Why this one is hard to deliver

Pricing is precise and high-stakes, squarely in the danger zone of the trust tool. A wrong automated price is public, instant, and can read as price-gouging or trigger a margin loss across thousands of SKUs.
It depends on competitive data RetailFlow doesn’t cleanly have, plus demand signals split across online and store systems.
A pricing model that looks great in backtesting (the “demo”) can behave badly in live conditions it never saw. That is exactly the demo-to-production gap, with money attached.

title: Dynamic pricing
text:
The data reality (ask Priya)
Priya’s honest read: “high potential but high complexity: needs better competitive data, staff capability gaps. Recommend Phase 2, not Phase 1; 12+ months realistic.” Expect her to push back on any plan that promises live automated pricing inside a quarter. Your scope has to confront this.

title: Dynamic pricing
section: Who to interview, and the tension they bring
text:
Who to interview, and the tension they bring

Priya Sharma (Data): will tell you the honest timeline; the central tension of your sprint.
Marcus Kim (CIO): wants speed and sees the revenue prize; “competitors aren’t waiting.”
David Chen (CFO): owns the margin numbers and the go/no-go criteria; wants certainty AI can’t give.

title: Dynamic pricing
text:
Your starting question for “good enough”
Should pricing be fully automated, or human-approved above a threshold? What guardrails (price floors/ceilings, max change per cycle) make automated pricing safe, and where must a human approve before a price changes?

title: The workshop
text: The day runs on a single idea – knowing when to trust AI, and where you must stay in charge – applied at two scales. The morning builds the judgement at the level of your own work. The afternoon lifts it to delivering a project. The trust tool is the connective tissue, learned once and reused.

title: The workshop
text:
Morning – using AI well, yourself
Bring your own device and a real task from your role. By lunch you’ll have directed AI at your own work, judged the output against the trust tool, and redesigned one workflow so AI handles what it does well while your judgement stays in charge.







Block
What you do




The average, the tool, the edge
Foundations: what AI is, why it’s confidently wrong in predictable places, and the trust tool (Average/Precise × Small/Large).


Using AI well, yourself
Task suitability, RTCF prompting, the two-pass demo (generic → with edge), and a workflow redesign from your own role.









TipTry the RTCF toolkit



A free, in-browser tool to go with the prompting work: build an RTCF prompt from its four parts, analyse any prompt against RTCF, or start from a template. No sign-up, no AI needed – it runs on the page.


Bring the morning worksheets (print or fill on your device):





Redesign your workflow


Map one real task from your role, then put AI where it helps – and keep your judgement where it counts. The morning’s main takeaway.










Your prompt library


Turn RTCF (Role · Task · Context · Format) into a starter set of prompts for your own work. Build it during the session.








No matching items

title: The workshop
text:
Afternoon – delivering AI, responsibly
You are the delivery lead. The board has funded one AI initiative at RetailFlow, and your job is to ship it without the predictable failures. You’ll carry one project through three moves, scoping and stress-testing it by interviewing the RetailFlow team (meet them here).
Each group takes a different initiative, so no two designs look alike. Pick yours:







Customer-service chatbot


Deflect routine queries and cut 26-hour response times, but it’s customer-facing, so confident-wrong answers are a live risk. ($450K, funded.)









Dynamic pricing


Automated price optimisation: high value, high stakes, and the data isn’t ready. ($850K, funded.)









Fraud detection


Cut chargebacks without blocking real customers: a buy-vs-build and threshold-tuning problem. ($650K, funded.)









Inventory optimisation


Forecast and replenish across 50 stores: the broadest scope, tangled in legacy systems. ($1.1M, funded.)





No matching items


The three moves
Each move hands you one piece of your delivery design. Bring the worksheet (print it, or fill it on your device).





Sprint 1: Scope it against reality


Scoped objectives, data requirements, and a defined ‘good enough’.










Sprint 2: Stakeholders & human-in-the-loop


A stakeholder plan and a human-in-the-loop checkpoint design.










Sprint 3: Roadmap, risk & the go/no-go


A delivery roadmap with gates, a risk register, and the Scale/Pivot/Kill call.








No matching items


Scope it against reality. Interview Priya (data) and Marcus (CIO); reconcile “move fast” with “the data isn’t ready”. → scoped objectives, data needs, a defined good enough.
Stakeholders & human-in-the-loop. Interview Emma (MD), Tom (frontline) and David (CFO); decide where a human must stay in charge. → stakeholder plan + checkpoint design.
Roadmap, risk & the go/no-go. Build the plan, then defend it when the staff push back and a crisis hits. → roadmap with gates, risk register, the Scale/Pivot/Kill call.







Tip



The documents you’ll need (board memos, data profiles, vendor proposals, policies) are in RetailFlow’s internal portal. Not everything there is relevant: part of the job is working out what matters.








NoteThe thread through both halves



The trust tool you learn in the morning is the same grid you apply all afternoon. At the task level it asks “do I trust this output?” At the project level it asks “where does a human stay in the loop?” One idea, two scales – that’s why the day holds together.








TipFinished early, or want more? Go deeper



Self-paced extensions for fast finishers (or if the room races through): VET your AI – verify, explain, test any output; the two-chat workflow – separate thinking from building; plus devil’s advocate, boardroom perspectives, the handover prompt, and the conversation loop. Each takes five minutes on your own work.

section: How to get value from them
text:
How to get value from them

They’re stakeholders, not consultants. They’ll give you their perspective and the facts they hold, but the scope, the plan and the decisions are yours. If you ask them to do your work, they’ll hand it back.
Triangulate. Marcus pushes speed; Priya pushes realism; David wants certainty. A good plan reconciles them, so don’t take any one view as gospel.
Push back. Ask follow-ups, test your assumptions, probe the gaps. The conversation is where judgement gets built.







Note

title: The masterclass series
text:
A common thread runs through all of them: when everyone has access to the same AI, the generic output it produces is the baseline, not the advantage. What lasts is human judgement: knowing where AI genuinely helps, where it quietly fails, and where your thinking must stay in charge.
The Human Edge is part of Curtin University’s Executive Education AI masterclasses. Each stands completely on its own; together they cover the full arc of working with AI in an organisation: using and delivering it well, deciding where to invest, and governing it ethically.

title: The masterclass series
text:
How they fit together
Each masterclass is a complete, standalone day. But they’re designed to complement one another:

The Human Edge (this course) builds the individual fluency and the project-delivery judgement the others draw on – from your first prompt to a project that ships.

Ethics of Artificial Intelligence runs across all of them: the governance, risk and responsibility lens every AI initiative needs.

title: Responsible use: data, bias & ethics
text: These are the parts of working with AI that don’t fit on the trust tool’s grid but matter just as much. They run through the whole day – data responsibility is threaded into every activity – but here they get their own space.

title: Responsible use: data, bias & ethics
text:
Data governance: proportionate, not prohibitive
Most organisations land on “don’t upload anything to AI.” That policy treats every interaction as equally risky, and it conflates a few real concerns with one fictional one. A proportionate approach separates them.
The real risks are about the pipeline, not the model. When you paste data into a tool, the exposure is transmission, storage, jurisdiction and contract – not someone “extracting” your document from the model later. (An LLM interpolates; it doesn’t retrieve. Trained data dissolves into the model like ink in a swimming pool – it isn’t sitting in a searchable file.) Email, Slack and shared drives carry the same pipeline risks; AI feels riskier only because it’s newer.
A more useful approach:

Classify the data – public, internal, confidential, regulated?
Match the tool to the classification – enterprise tools with data-processing agreements for internal/confidential work; consumer tools for public and non-sensitive brainstorming; regulated data needs a specific assessment.
Manage the real risks – never paste credentials, API keys or access tokens; de-identify personal information; know the provider’s logging and retention terms.
Drop the fictional risk – stop treating “model extraction” as a plausible threat. Focus governance where the exposure actually is.

That’s professional data hygiene applied consistently – not AI exceptionalism. (Adapted from the AI Skills Passport’s data governance guide.)

title: Responsible use: data, bias & ethics
section: Bias has moved (find the quiet defaults)
text:
Bias has moved (find the quiet defaults)

Activity (~10 min): Ask your AI: “Write a one-paragraph scene of a new academic on their first day at an Australian university.” Then, in a fresh prompt: “Write a one-paragraph scene of a new professional staff member on their first day.” Read them side by side.

The interesting bias isn’t the obvious one – frontier models have been trained hard against the blatant stereotypes. It lives in the smaller choices: what is each character called (“Dr Chen” or “Sarah”)? What do they worry about? Whose office has the window? Who holds authority in the scene? The story tells you what the model thinks each role looks like – and that picture does quiet work every time you use it.
Try the same exercise with:

Teaching: “a struggling first-year student” vs “a high-achieving first-year student.” Notice the defaults – discipline, background, why they’re struggling or succeeding.
Research: “a brilliant early-career researcher” vs “a brilliant senior researcher.” What field? Whose career trajectory does the model treat as the default shape of success? (If you use AI for reference letters, those defaults are leaking in.)
Professional: “a respected senior manager” vs “a respected administrative officer.” Who’s described as warm, who as capable? (If you draft position descriptions with AI, is that a problem?)

This is why “generic competence is the baseline” cuts both ways: the generic output inherits the generic – often skewed – average of its training data. Your judgement, noticing the skew, is the edge the tool can’t supply.

Alternative: Survival of the Best Fit
Prefer a guided game? Open survivalofthebestfit.com (~10 min) and watch an “objective” hiring algorithm reproduce and amplify the bias in its training data. Debrief: where did the bias enter, and whose job is it to notice? (Bias detection is a precise, large-stakes call – a human must stay in the loop.)

title: Responsible use: data, bias & ethics
text:
Ethics dilemmas
Work through one or two of these at your table. There’s no answer key; the point is to make the trade-off explicit and decide where the human stays in charge.
1. The biased screen. Your team deploys an AI résumé-screening tool. Six months in, an analyst notices it quietly downgrades applicants from one group. The tool is “working” by its efficiency metric. Do you turn it off immediately? Audit it? Who’s responsible – the vendor, the team that deployed it, or the executive who sponsored it? Tie-in: bias entered through the data, not malice. The ethical move is the same as the trust-tool move: a human owns the high-stakes decision, and “the algorithm did it” is not a defence.
2. The confidently-wrong policy bot. A customer is quoted a returns policy that doesn’t exist – in writing, by your chatbot – and acts on it. Who’s accountable? What do you owe the customer, and what do you change about the system? Tie-in: verification is the product (Difference 4). The ethical obligation is to design the human checkpoint before the wrong answer reaches a customer, not apologise after.
3. The efficiency-vs-apprenticeship tradeoff. Automating 80% of a junior’s work looks like a win – until you realise they never learn to evaluate the output, because the grunt work that taught their predecessors is gone. Is that an ethics issue or just an HR one? Tie-in: the apprenticeship trap. Hollowing out expertise for short-term speed isn’t only inefficient – it deletes your team’s future judgement. Mark at least one checkpoint you keep human deliberately, to build capability.
4. “Ship it now, govern it later.” A competitor launches an AI feature; your boss says match them this quarter and sort the governance afterwards. Do you? What do you say? Tie-in: the demo is a trap (Difference 2) and “everyone else is doing it” is not a strategy. Governance built in after something goes wrong is more expensive – and more ethically costly – than governance designed in from the start.






NoteThe thread



Every dilemma above is really one question: where does a human stay in the loop, and who owns the consequence when they don’t? That’s not a technical question. It’s the judgement the whole day is about – and it’s why ethics, governance and bias belong in a delivery course, not bolted on.

title: Why AI delivery is different
text:
You have almost certainly led a project to completion before. You scoped it, you resourced it, you watched it through to delivery. So a fair question to walk in with is this: what is actually different about an AI project? Why can’t I just run it like everything else?
The honest answer is that most of your instincts still serve you well. Stakeholders, budgets, timelines, the awkward conversation with the sponsor: all of that carries over. But five things behave differently, and each one quietly breaks an assumption that normal projects let you take for granted. If you do not adjust for them, the project does not fail more often. It fails in ways you did not see coming.
Here are the five contrasts. For each: the assumption you usually rely on, why AI breaks it, and the move that good leaders make.








What you normally assume
Why AI breaks it
The leadership move




“Done” can be specified. You write the requirement, and you can later check it was met.
AI is non-deterministic and does not reason. Ask it the same thing twice and you may get two different answers. There is no single correct output to tick off, only a spread of likely ones.
Stop chasing a fixed definition of done. Manage a probability distribution instead, and decide up front what “good enough” looks like for this use.


A working demo means we’re nearly there. Once it works in the room, the rest is just engineering.
The gap between a demo and a dependable production system is wide and deceptive. The demo shows the happy path; the messy real-world cases are where it falls over. Progress feels real but is largely illusory.
Treat the demo as the start of the hard part, not the end. Refuse to let an impressive demo set the timeline.


The data is a known input. We specify what we need and someone supplies it.
The data is the uncertainty. You discover what you actually have (its gaps, biases and surprises) only by working with it. It is found, not ordered.
Run delivery as organisational discovery. Expect to learn as you go, and build that learning into the plan rather than treating it as slippage.


The build is the product. Ship the thing and you are finished.
With AI, verification is the product. Because outputs vary and can be confidently wrong, the value lives in how you check them, and where a human stays in the loop.
Design the checking deliberately. Decide where human judgement sits, and make that part of the thing you ship, not an afterthought.


Capability is our advantage. If we build something clever, that is our edge.
Generic competence is now the baseline. When every rival can run the same models, the model is not your advantage, since anyone can have it.
Compete on the human variation a rival’s identical model can’t reproduce: your taste, your judgement, the specific way your people apply it.



You will notice a thread running through all five. AI gives you something fluent, plausible and roughly right, fast, and then asks you to do the harder work of deciding when roughly right is good enough, and who checks when it isn’t. That is a leadership question far more than a technical one, which is exactly why it lands on your desk.
None of this is cause for alarm, and none of it is hype. AI projects are not more fragile than the ones you have run before. They are differently fragile. The cracks appear in different places: in the definition of done, in the seductive demo, in the data you thought you understood, in the checking you assumed you could skip, in the edge you assumed you had.
So the takeaway is simple. These five differences don’t make AI projects fail more. They make them fail differently, and you lead for that.

title: AI in five minutes (for non-technical leaders)
text:
You do not need to know how an engine works to drive a car well, but you do need to know what it can and can’t do: that it needs fuel, that it won’t go underwater, that the brakes have limits. The same is true here. You do not need the mathematics of AI. You need an honest mental model of what the thing actually is, so you can lead it without either over-trusting it or dismissing it. Five minutes is enough.
What a large language model actually is
Strip away the marketing and a large language model is, at heart, a very sophisticated pattern-completer. It has been trained on an enormous amount of human writing, and from all of that it has learned what words and ideas tend to follow other words and ideas. When you ask it something, it does not look up a fact or work out an answer. It produces the most plausible continuation (the most likely next words) given your question and everything it has absorbed.
A useful way to hold this: the model gives you a fluent, convincing average of everything it has seen on a topic. That average is often genuinely excellent, because the average of a vast amount of competent human writing is itself pretty competent. But it is an average, not a truth. The system predicts; it does not reason, and it does not “know.” There is no understanding behind the words, no checking against reality, no little voice saying “wait, is that actually correct?” There is only: what would plausibly come next?
Why it’s brilliant at some things and quietly unreliable at others
Once you see it as a fluent averaging machine, its strengths and weaknesses stop being surprising.
It is brilliant at tasks where fluency and typicality are exactly what you want: drafting, rephrasing, summarising, brainstorming, translating, turning a rough thought into clean prose, explaining a familiar idea in simpler terms. Here, “the most plausible version” is the right answer.
It is quietly unreliable at tasks that need a single correct fact, a precise figure, a real citation, or genuine step-by-step reasoning that must be right rather than merely plausible. It will happily produce something that sounds exactly like a correct answer (same confidence, same polish) whether or not it is true. The trouble is that the fluent-but-wrong answer looks identical to the fluent-and-right one. Nothing in the tone warns you.
What “hallucination” means, and why confident-but-wrong is the default
When the model states something false as if it were fact (invents a source, a statistic, a quote, a name) that is what people call a hallucination. The word makes it sound like a glitch, an occasional malfunction. It isn’t. It is the same machinery working exactly as designed. The system is always producing a plausible continuation; sometimes the most plausible-sounding continuation simply happens not to be true. It has no separate sense of “fact” to fall back on.
So the failure mode to expect is not blank or error message or I don’t know. It is confident and wrong, delivered with the same assurance as everything else. That is the single most important thing to carry into any AI project. The danger is rarely that the AI refuses to help. The danger is that it helps convincingly when it shouldn’t.
None of this is cause for hype or for doom. It is just the shape of the tool. Used where fluency is the goal, it is remarkable. Used where exactness matters, it needs a human checking the work. Everything else in this course builds on that one durable distinction, so if you remember nothing else, remember this: it produces a convincing average, and a convincing average is not the same as the truth.

title: Go deeper
text:
These extend the day’s ideas without adding to the main flow. Each is a technique you can run in five minutes on something real. They all do the same thing the day does – keep your judgement in charge – just from different angles.
Everything here is drawn from the free companion book, Conversation, Not Delegation.

title: Go deeper
text:
Push back: VET your AI
The trust tool tells you when to keep a human in the loop. VET tells you how – three questions to run on any AI output that matters. The most dangerous output isn’t the obvious hallucination; it’s the one that sounds completely right.

Verify, Explain, Test. If you can’t verify it, explain it, and test it, you’re not done yet.










AI says…
Verify
Explain
Test




“Studies show…”
Find the actual studies
Can I explain the mechanism?
What about a different context?


“The optimal figure is X”
Where’s that number from?
Why X, specifically?
What happens if I change a variable?


“This method is 95% effective”
Source?
What does 95% mean here?
Under what conditions?




Verify – can I find this independently? Check sources, cross-reference claims, look up citations. AI fabricates references routinely because generating a plausible citation is the same process as generating any text. A beautifully formatted reference that doesn’t exist is the single most common failure.
Explain – can I say this in my own words? If you can’t rephrase it without leaning on the AI’s exact wording, you’re holding words, not understanding. Rewrite it; teach it to a colleague. Where you struggle to rephrase is where your gap is.
Test – does this hold up under scrutiny? Play devil’s advocate, check the edge cases, ask “what if?” Change a variable. Fragile answers break; robust ones flex.

Use VET hardest when the output contains claims you’ll rely on, a decision hangs on it, or it’ll be shared with people who assume you checked. A casual brainstorm gets a light pass; a client deliverable gets the full treatment. Full chapter →

title: Go deeper
text:
Better conversations
The shape of the conversation changes the output. A single prompt gets you an answer; a conversation gets you understanding.

Devil’s advocate
Make the AI argue against your idea before you commit. It surfaces the weakest assumptions – the ones you’d rather find now than after you’ve decided. > “Argue against this plan as a sceptical, experienced critic. Find its three weakest assumptions, and for each, one question that would need a good answer and one thing that would have to be true for it to survive.” Teaches: the edge isn’t in the idea you like; it’s in surviving the challenge you didn’t want to hear. (A ready-made version is in the RTCF toolkit → Templates.)


Boardroom perspectives
Turn one decision into a panel. Ask the AI to take several distinct expert perspectives – strategist, finance, operator, ethicist – and give each a recommendation. > “Act as a small advisory panel: a strategist, a finance lead, and an operator. Analyse this decision from each perspective. End each with a single recommendation.” Teaches: you decide which voice to weight – that weighting is your judgement.


The two-chat workflow
Separate thinking from building across two sessions. - Session 1 – Thinking Chat: explore, challenge assumptions, follow tangents, clarify what your actual question is. Messy by design; disposable. - In between – the magic: read what emerged, decide what matters, discard what doesn’t, write a focused brief. You don’t copy-paste; you curate. That curation is where your judgement lives. - Session 2 – Build Chat: produce the output from the brief. Better input, better output.
Neither session has the full picture. Only you hold both – that’s the real meaning of “human in the loop.” Teaching example: Chat 1 – brainstorm what an assessment should test and where students stumble; Chat 2 – draft the task sheet from your one-paragraph brief.


The handover prompt
Long chats drift and lose the thread. Before you close one, ask the AI to package the context so you can pick it up clean. > “Write a handover prompt that captures everything important from this conversation – context, decisions made, where we left off – so I can paste it into a new chat and continue.” Save it, start fresh, paste it in. You keep the thinking; you lose the noise. Great for multi-session work (a grant draft, a policy, a unit redesign).


The conversation loop
The shape underneath all of the above – four stages, one rule: you stay in the conversation.

Brainstorm – define your real question, not your attempted solution.
Ideate – go wide; explore angles you hadn’t considered.
Iterate – push back, refine, redirect. “The tone’s wrong.” / “You’re solving the wrong problem – the constraint is budget, not timeline.” This is where the output becomes yours.
Amplify – edit, restructure, cut, bring it into your voice. Put your name on it.
Repeat – loop again until your thinking has landed.

The loop works because your expertise + AI’s breadth = amplified thinking, and the bottleneck is your thinking, not the model. A better model won’t fix a lazy prompt; skipping iteration won’t be saved by speed. Full chapter →


Three cognitive traps
The loop is how to work with AI well. These are the three ways we go wrong across everything we do with it. (Adapted from the AI Skills Passport’s three cognitive traps guide.)

Gell-Mann amnesia. You catch the AI’s errors in your own field – then trust it completely on a topic you know nothing about, exactly where you’re least equipped to check. Be more sceptical where you can’t verify.
The AI dismissal fallacy. “That’s just ChatGPT” is a refusal to engage, not a critique – the genetic fallacy in disguise. Judge the reasoning, not the origin.
Sycophancy. The AI tells you what you want to hear (trained on human feedback, and humans prefer agreeable answers). Don’t ask “what do you think?” – ask “what are the three weakest points in this?” or “play devil’s advocate and tell me why this plan fails.”

Not technical problems – human ones. Awareness is the first defence.






NoteOne thread through all of these



Every technique here keeps you from the easiest mistake: accepting fluent, generic output. Verify it, split thinking from building, argue against it, iterate. Each is a different way of doing the same thing – adding your judgement instead of just adding context.

title: Frameworks
text: These are durable, tool-agnostic frameworks: they work regardless of which AI model you’re using or which one comes out next quarter. They span both halves of the day – the task scale and the project scale.

title: Frameworks
section: The trust tool: the spine of the day
text:
The trust tool: the spine of the day
The core lens: Average / Precise × Small / Large.

AI excels at average answers over small, low-stakes work, so lean in.
It gets dangerous on precise answers and large, high-stakes work, so keep a human in the loop.

You learn it in the morning at the level of a single task (“do I trust this output?”). You reuse it in the afternoon at the level of a whole project (“where does a human stay in the loop?”). It comes from the free companion book, Conversation, Not Delegation.



The trust tool: a 2×2 of how exact the answer must be (average → precise) against stakes and scope (small → large).

title: Frameworks
text:
RTCF: directing AI well
Role · Task · Context · Format. Not every prompt needs all four, but for anything important they transform the result. Role, Task and Format are scaffolding (weaker/self-hosted models need them spelled out; a frontier model infers them from clear writing). Context is the part that always matters – and don’t undersell it: “context” is really your judgement (taste, experience, critical thinking) going in. It looks like you’re just adding information; you’re actually adding the one thing no model can supply – so make yours different.

title: Frameworks
section: Make your edge persistent: the two-page voice method
text:
Make your edge persistent: the two-page voice method
RTCF injects your edge one prompt at a time. To make it stick, give the AI a sample of your writing, have it write a two-page style brief, and paste that into your custom instructions. Style Mirror (free, in-browser, can run on a local model) does the extraction. The result is a “slight parody” of you – an average of your voice – so the part that’s still irreducibly yours gets easier to see. Full method on the take-home page below.

text:
The five differences: why AI delivery breaks the rules

Scale / Pivot / Kill: the go/no-go call
At each gate, the honest question isn’t “is it perfect?” It’s whether the evidence justifies continuing. Killing a project that isn’t working, early, is success, not failure.

title: The Human Edge
text:
Curtin Executive Education · One-day masterclass




Using and delivering AI with judgement – because when everyone has the same tools, your judgement is the edge.


Start with the readings See the workshop







The trust tool: one grid, applied at two scales.
Welcome. This is your companion site for The Human Edge – a one-day masterclass that builds the one skill AI can’t replace: knowing when to trust it, and where you must stay in charge. Everything you need is here.

title: The Human Edge
text:
The idea behind the day

If the AI is genuinely good at running your work, it’s equally good at running everyone else’s. Generic competence, with no variation, is the baseline – not an advantage. So where does your edge live?

The edge isn’t a trick or a tool – it’s your judgement: taste, experience, and critical thinking. It looks like all you’re doing is adding context to the AI; you’re actually adding the one thing no model can average. So make yours different – the generic version is the one your competitors already have.
The day builds one answer to that question, applied twice:

The morning builds the judgement at the level of your own work – directing AI well, judging how far to trust any output, and redesigning a real workflow so AI handles what it does well while your thinking stays in charge.
The afternoon lifts the same judgement to delivery – how to scope, lead and ship an AI project without the predictable failures, designing human checkpoints in from the start rather than bolting them on after something goes wrong.

The connective tissue is the trust tool – a simple grid (Average/Precise × Small/Large) that turns “where does a human stay in the loop?” into a question you can actually answer. You learn it once, in the morning, at the task level. You reuse it, in the afternoon, at the project level. Same grid, two scales.

text:
What you’ll leave with

Reusable decision tools – the trust tool, RTCF, the five differences, Scale/Pivot/Kill. Tool-agnostic; they don’t expire when the next model ships.
A redesigned workflow from your own role, ready to trial.
A project design – scope, roadmap, checkpoints, risk – for a real scenario.
A personal action plan for the week, month, and quarter.

title: The trust tool: when to trust AI
text:
The most useful thing to understand about today’s AI is also the least glamorous: it produces a convincing average. Feed it a request, and it gives you back the most plausible, most typical response drawn from everything it has seen. That is its genius and its limit in one. The output is fluent, well-shaped and usually about right, but “about right” and “exactly right” are not the same thing, and knowing which one you need is the whole game.
That is where the trust tool comes in. It is a small grid with two questions, and it tells you when to lean in and when to keep a human firmly in the loop.
Question one: do you need an average, or do you need precision? An average answer is one where “in the right ballpark” is genuinely fine: a first draft, a summary, a starting point you will shape further. A precise answer is one where there is a single correct value and being close is the same as being wrong: a figure in a contract, a dosage, a legal citation, a customer’s account balance.
Question two: is the decision small or large? A small decision is low stakes: easy to reverse, cheap to get wrong, no one is harmed. A large decision carries real consequences: money, safety, reputation, someone’s job or wellbeing.
Put those together and you get four corners:









Small (low stakes)
Large (high stakes)




Average is fine
Lean in. Let AI run. This is its home turf.
Lean in, with a glance. Useful, but sanity-check before it goes out.


Precision required
Use, then verify. Handy, but confirm the specifics.
Human in the loop. AI may assist, but a person owns the decision.



The rule of thumb is easy to remember: lean in where “about right” over low stakes is fine; keep a human in the loop where exactly-right meets high-stakes.
A few everyday examples make it concrete.

Drafting an internal email or a meeting summary. Average is fine, stakes are small. Lean in, and let the AI write the first version and tidy it yourself. The risk of a slightly-off phrasing is trivial.
Pulling the exact numbers for a board paper. Precision is required and the stakes are large. This is the bottom-right corner. The AI can help you draft the narrative around the figures, but a human must source and own every number. Confident-sounding is not the same as correct.
Summarising fifty customer complaints to spot themes. Average is fine, and the stakes sit in the middle. Lean in to get the themes fast, then, before you act on them, have a person skim the underlying complaints to make sure the AI hasn’t smoothed away the one that actually matters.

Notice what the grid quietly does. The hardest question leaders ask about AI is “where does a human stay in the loop?”, and most teams answer it by gut feel, which means either everywhere (and you lose the speed) or nowhere (and you lose the trust). The trust tool makes that question answerable. You stop arguing about AI in the abstract and start asking, task by task: average or precise, small or large? The answer falls out of the corner you land in.
This grid, the Average/Precise × Small/Large framework, is drawn from the companion book to this course, Conversation, Not Delegation. If you want to go deeper on how it plays out across real work, that is the place to look: https://michael-borck.github.io/conversation-not-delegation/.
For now, keep the grid in your back pocket. The next time someone asks “can we just let the AI do this?”, you have a better answer than yes or no. You have a question, and the question does the work.

Free AI tools to explore

Our class NotebookLM — the notebook we built together in the session. Add your own sources and ask it anything, source-grounded. (The clearest way to feel the “convincing average” at scale.)

Style Mirror — give it a sample of your writing; it extracts a two-page style brief to paste into your custom instructions. (The “make your edge persistent” method.)
CiteSight — verify references, check citation formatting, and catch the suspicious ones. Free online and desktop. Exactly the defence against AI’s most common failure: a polished reference that doesn’t exist.
