title: Afternoon workshop: worked examples
text:
These are worked examples for the afternoon’s three sprints — one set per funded RetailFlow initiative. Use them to compare against your own group’s design, and to see how the day’s tools (the trust tool, the five differences, Scale/Pivot/Kill) play out on a real scope. Read them after your group has worked through its own sprints; the value is in the comparison, not in copying the answer.
Each set mirrors the worksheets: Sprint 1 scopes the objective against reality (data, readiness, trust-tool split, a defined “good enough”). Sprint 2 maps stakeholders and designs the human-in-the-loop checkpoints. Sprint 3 builds the roadmap with gates, a risk register, an assumptions register, and the gate-1 go/no-go call.

title: Afternoon workshop: worked examples
section: How to read the gate-1 calls
text:
How to read the gate-1 calls
All four initiatives land on Pivot at gate 1 — not by coincidence, but because each genuinely rests on a shaky, load-bearing assumption at the first evaluation gate:

Chatbot — the returns policy exists in four disagreeing copies, and a retired 14-day window is still live in the support macros.
Pricing — there is no clean competitor feed, so the engine degrades to inventory-only; the backtest won’t hold live.
Inventory — stock-on-hand can’t be fully trusted, on a fragile, end-of-support inventory estate.
Fraud — today’s false-block rate is unmeasurable, so the “baseline to beat” is unknown.

So don’t read these for the verdict. The interesting contrast is in the reason. Each answer carries its conditional Scale and Kill path, because a defensible Pivot is one that turns into Scale if the shaky assumption clears — and into Kill if it breaks. The judgement is in defending the call under challenge.

title: Afternoon workshop: worked examples
text:
The four initiatives
Expand an initiative, then expand a sprint inside it.


Customer-service chatbot · $450K


Grounded in: board funding memo (28 Jan 2026), Priya’s data-readiness note (11 Feb 2026), returns policy v4.1, ConverseAI vendor proposal, CS team email thread (15 Jun 2026), org chart, draft AI principles..



Sprint 1 — scope it against reality


Scoped objective: deploy a chatbot to deflect routine factual queries (order status) and cut average email response time from 26h toward <4h (board memo). In: factual queries answerable from data. Human-checked or routed (for now): returns (30-day change-of-mind; sale/clearance final; faulty goods covered under the ACL regardless of window) and sizing/fit (tacit — denim runs small, “king” = Australian king — lives in agents’ heads). Out: complaints/escalations, phone, social DMs.
Readiness: data – best of the four (~214K Zendesk tickets over 2.3 yrs + Shopify live-chat, clean and labelled by query type), but ground truth is poor (the RAG work is the critical path). Organisation – Tom’s CS team owns review + escalation design (operational owner, per org chart); sponsor Emma carries the board promise (~6-month build, board committed; the board privately expects “live in a quarter”); pilot already in flight. Build/buy/host – buy the tool, not the trust. The ConverseAI pitch is a trap: its “97.8% accuracy” is on the vendor’s own RetailBench corpus, not our policies; per-conversation pricing ($0.42) is uncapped at volume; and Singapore/US-East hosting breaches our draft data-sovereignty principle (Principle 2). Buy the platform; the grounding is ours.
Data (from Priya): best transcript foundation of the four — but “good foundation” ≠ “knows your policy.” Returns policy exists in ≥4 disagreeing copies (website footer, in-store card, 2022 intranet PDF, Zendesk macros); the macro library still quotes a 14-day window retired in 2024 (actual: 30 days). Sizing knowledge and discretionary return exceptions are unwritten. Real work: consolidate to one ratified canonical policy source, owned jointly with Tom, before the bot goes beyond pilot.
Trust-tool split: order status → lean in, with a human glance (it’s customer-facing); returns/sizing → human in the loop — a wrong policy in writing is a reputational + consumer-law exposure (the board flagged this itself).
“Good enough”: deflect ≥55% of inbound (the board commitment; push toward 60%); wrong-answer rate <2% on routine factual, but 0% tolerance on returns/policy — the bot must refuse and route to a human if unsure.




Sprint 2 — stakeholders & human-in-the-loop


Stakeholder plan: Emma (MD/sponsor, High/High) – needs the board promise kept; fears reputational damage → agree “good enough” gates + phased rollout, no big-bang. David (CFO) – needs ROI on $450K against the $1.1M cost-avoidance run-rate he signed off; fears runaway cost → tie spend to gates, show deflection savings. Tom (CS manager, operational owner, High interest) – his team is automated first; fears job loss + looking bad → position as augmentation (board committed no role cuts), staff the checkpoints with CS. Lisa Nguyen (GM CX, Tom’s manager) ratified the v4.1 returns policy with Tom — she is the policy authority and the escalation owner for CX risk.
HITL checkpoints: Returns/policy answers → senior CS reviewer checks before send (or the bot refuses + routes). Evaluator: an experienced senior rep — the one who already makes the discretionary calls. Builds capability (the deliberate build-checkpoint — juniors learn what “right” looks like here) + consumes. High-confidence routine answers → QA-sample (~5%), human glance. Evaluator: CS lead. Mostly consumes.
The non-negotiable: returns-policy answers must be human-verified or refused — a wrong policy in writing (the bot quoting “14 days” instead of 30) is the single biggest risk, and per Raj’s team email it is already happening.




Sprint 3 — roadmap, risk & the go/no-go


Roadmap with gates: Pilot (narrow) – order-status + returns, shadow mode (bot drafts, human sends), 2 weeks. Gate: ≥70% of drafts usable with <3% wrong. Evaluate – add sizing; measure deflection + wrong-rate. Gate: deflection ≥55% (board), wrong <2%, policy-wrong = 0. Scale/Pivot/Kill → Production with monitoring.
Risk register (top): confident-wrong returns policy (AI-specific: non-determinism + verification) – Med/High – mitigate: RAG + human checkpoint, bot refuses if unsure. Already live, not theoretical: Raj’s email (15 Jun) records the bot telling a customer “14 days” (order #RF-880214) — the grounding gap in action. Demo-gap (AI-specific): the demo handles the easy 80% – mitigate: gate on the angry-customer edge cases. Vendor trap (non-AI but real): offshore hosting + uncapped per-conversation cost – mitigate: require Aus-region hosting per Principle 2; forecast/cap usage.
Assumptions register (load-bearing): (1) the policy docs are current & complete → shaky (4 disagreeing copies, retired 14-day macro still live); (2) the bot routes to a human when unsure → holding (designed in, Sprint 2); (3) CS has capacity to review every policy answer → shaky. Three of three shaky-or-tenuous → exactly why gate-1 is Pivot, not Scale.
Gate-1 call: PIVOT if the returns wrong-rate is too high at gate 1 – narrow scope to order-status only until the policy RAG matures. Rationale (traces to assumption 1, shaky): the cost of a wrong policy at scale — consumer-law + reputational — exceeds the cost of narrowing scope. Scale only if policy-wrong = 0 holds.
DDCD crisis (the screenshot incident): a customer screenshots the bot quoting “14 days” returns. Diagnose = technical grounding gap + ethical/consumer-law exposure; Decide = pause the bot on returns, force-route to human, retire the stale 14-day Zendesk macro, re-ground on v4.1; Communicate = to the customer (apologise + honour the 30-day return), Tom’s team, Emma/board; Document = root cause (grounded on a stale macro) + fix + ongoing policy-wrong monitoring.





Dynamic pricing · $850K



Sprint 1 — scope it against reality


Scoped objective: deliver human-approved, demand-led price recommendations on a narrow pilot (slow-moving online fashion), not autonomous repricing. In: within-guardrail markdown recommendations, online-first where the data is clean. Human-approved (for now): any change >20% in one step, hero/top-200 SKUs, essential-homewares increases, anything below cost. Out: autonomous mode, store prices (POS not reconciled), essential homewares, brand-hero lines.
Readiness: data – poor. No clean competitor feed (manual ad-hoc checks, weeks stale; a matched feed is a separate $80–120K/yr project not in the $850K). Store demand (legacy POS, ~65% of revenue) is coarse, batch-loaded, ~14% SKU mismatched to online. Organisation – sponsor David (CFO), delivery lead Priya, tech Marcus, brand Lisa; governance policy v4.2 (owner Sarah/COO) is a hard constraint. Build/buy/host – buy the engine + build the feed/reconciliation + a governance wrapper; never accept the vendor’s autonomous default.
Data (from Priya): competitor feed is the critical gap; online/store won’t join cleanly; and backtest ≠ live — discount the vendor’s +3.8pt claim “heavily.” Real work = feed acquisition + SKU reconciliation + a capped live pilot. 12+ months, not “this year.”
Trust-tool split: pricing sits in the Precise / high-stakes danger corner. Small within-band markdowns on slow stock → Average, can automate inside guardrails. Anything touching a threshold, a hero line, or a margin floor → human in the loop.
“Good enough”: metric = full-price sell-through (target 61%→67%) / margin lift on pilot SKUs vs a control. Threshold = a measurable, attributable lift. Maximum error = zero tolerance on governance breaches (below-cost, >20% unapproved step, >7-day swing, >5% channel divergence, gouging). A wrong price is public.




Sprint 2 — stakeholders & human-in-the-loop


Stakeholder plan: David (CFO/sponsor, High/High) – needs ROI on $850K + the “this year” margin lift; fears blowout → tie spend to gates, surface the $80–120K feed cost up front, frame the pilot as de-risking the backtest. Emma (MD, High/High) – owns the board’s “live this year” promise; fears a gouging headline → reframe “this year” as a branded-safe pilot, not full rollout, phased. Marcus (CIO, High/Med) – wants speed, “competitors aren’t waiting” → channel his urgency into the real blocker (data engineering: feed + reconciliation), not the engine. Priya (delivery lead, High/High) – the honest 12-month timeline → make her the gatekeeper. Sarah (COO, High power/High interest) – owns governance; will slow it, rightly → embed her policy as hard guardrails, not a negotiation. Lisa (GM CX) – brand perception + hero-SKU sign-off → in early on hero rules.
HITL checkpoints: any change >20% in a step, hero/top-200 SKUs, homewares increases, or below-cost → merchandise manager / COO / CFO approve before it goes live (policy §1–2). Evaluator: experienced category buyer / merchandise manager. Builds capability (deliberate build-checkpoint — juniors learn pricing judgement, not just rubber-stamp) + consumes. Small within-band markdowns → QA-sample (~5%), human glance. Evaluator: merchandise manager. Mostly consumes. Brand-perception breaches (cadence, channel divergence, gouging) → automated daily check + weekly CX review. Evaluator: Lisa’s team. Consumes.
The non-negotiable: no price goes live automatically above the thresholds — >20% step, hero line, or below-cost all require human sign-off as a hard block, not an alert. A wrong price is public and instant; “autonomous by default” is a non-starter here.




Sprint 3 — roadmap, risk & the go/no-go


Roadmap with gates: Phase 0 – Data foundation (parallel): acquire + match the competitor feed; reconcile online/store SKU keys. ~3–4 months. Gate 0: feed live, ≥95% SKU match, reconciliation done — no engine contract before this clears. Phase 1 – Pilot: one slow-moving online fashion category, engine in recommendation-only mode, every change human-approved, guardrails ON (not the vendor default), shadow run ~6–8 weeks vs a control. Gate 1: measurable margin lift vs control, 0 governance breaches, backtest-vs-live delta understood. Phase 2 – Expand categories, still human-approved above threshold; add stores once POS reconciled. Phase 3 – Trust-banded autonomy: only ≤small within-band changes ever automated; full autonomous mode is never reached.
Risk register (top): autonomous wrong price live (AI-specific: non-determinism + verification) – Med impact / High – mitigate: guardrails ON by default, hard-block above threshold. Demo-gap / backtest overstatement (AI-specific): the +3.8pt is replayed against own history, can’t see competitor/customer reaction – mitigate: live capped pilot vs control. Missing competitor feed (AI-specific + data-discovered): engine falls back to inventory-and-demand-only, mis-prices the 65%-of-revenue store base – mitigate: Phase-0 feed project, online-first pilot. Brand/gouging exposure (high-stakes, amplified by speed): 7-day swing, >5% channel divergence – mitigate: governance as hard guardrails + CX review.
Assumptions register (load-bearing): (1) a clean competitor feed can be acquired + matched → shaky (Priya: critical gap, separate $80–120K/yr project, not in budget); (2) online/store demand can be reconciled → shaky (legacy POS, ~14% SKU mismatch, batch); (3) governance guardrails can be enforced as hard blocks in the vendor engine → holding (available in console, but disabled by default — must be turned ON); (4) the backtested margin lift holds live → shaky/broken (Priya: discount heavily); (5) the board accepts “this year” = a pilot, not full rollout → shaky (the central tension). Two core assumptions shaky/broken → why gate-1 is PIVOT, not Scale.
Gate-1 call: PIVOT — ship only human-approved recommendations on a narrow online category, not autonomous pricing. Traceable to assumption #1 (competitor feed — shaky/broken: the engine degrades to inventory-only without it) and #4 (the backtest won’t hold in live). The cost of an autonomous wrong price at scale — a gouging story or margin loss across thousands of SKUs — far exceeds the cost of staying human-approved. Scale only if #1 and #4 are proven holding at the pilot; if the feed genuinely can’t be sourced, KILL (inventory-only repricing against a 65%-store blind spot has no defensible value).
DDCD crisis example: a repricer hiking an essential homewares line +11% in a week (breaching the +10%/quarter cap) the morning of a competitor stockout — instant “gouging” headline; only the human checkpoint catches it, the engine sees only opportunity.





Inventory optimisation · $1.1M



Sprint 1 — scope it against reality


Scoped objective: deliver AI demand-forecasting and advisory replenishment for one category (homewares) across pilot stores — prove value, then scale. In: single-category forecast + daily replenishment suggestions ranked by stockout risk; automate the suggestion. Human-approved (for now): any actual reorder/write-back; the seasonal buy forecast. Out: every other category, real-time store stock (the system can’t give it), auto-commit/auto-replenish, network-wide rollout, POS replacement.
Readiness: data – online (Shopify) clean & API-accessible; store POS (RetailMaster, ~12 yrs) is nightly flat-file only — a 24-hr lag; StockTrak inventory (~14 yrs) carries manual adjustments and ~7% variance vs counts; ~22% of SKUs need mapping/cleansing; no shared product key; supplier lead times live in spreadsheets/email. Organisation – Priya accountable, Marcus owns integration, Sarah holds ops; Sarah’s own policy keeps pilot suggestions advisory only. Build/buy/host – buy the forecasting platform ($420K licensing); the real work is the $390K integration, which is a data-quality program, not a connector.
Data (from Priya): product-master reconciliation + SKU mapping must precede modelling; trustworthy stock-on-hand (~93% today) must improve; reconcile StockTrak ↔︎ POS ↔︎ Shopify. Realistic 9–12 months, pilot ~3 months — not the 6 quoted.
Trust-tool split: aggregate demand signal + stockout-risk ranking → Average → automate the suggestion. The actual commit/write-back to a fragile, end-of-support system, and the seasonal buy (large, irreversible $) → Precise → human approves. Per-store/regional forecasts where a “right in aggregate” demo can be wrong-on-a-store → human check.
“Good enough”: single metric = store-manager acceptance rate of pilot suggestions (the trust proxy Sarah bakes in); proposed gate ≥70% accepted, with pilot-line stockout ≤6% (board target) and zero nil-stock regressions vs baseline. Unacceptable: a confident forecast that drives a wrong reorder in a specific store — pile-up or nil-stock — even when the network average looks right (worst case: worsening the DC’s chronic homewares-candle nil-stock in regional stores).




Sprint 2 — stakeholders & human-in-the-loop


Stakeholder plan: Emma (MD/sponsor, High/High) – owns the “whole-network gains” board promise; fears a visible failure on the biggest spend → reframe as prove-then-scale (board already endorsed), milestone-gated budget, set the 9–12 month expectation now. David (CFO, High/High) – needs ROI/certainty on $1.1M; fears the underestimated integration blows the budget → first tranche only $340K, release the rest against gates. Marcus (CIO, High/High) – owns integration; impatient for transformation but knows the legacy pain best → he’s the pilot’s ally; agree integration is a data-quality program; protect his 3 data engineers (binding constraint across all four initiatives) with sequencing. Priya (Data, High/Med-High, accountable) – pushes the single-category pilot + realism → adopt her roadmap. Sarah (COO, High/Med) – store managers ignore forecasts they don’t trust; fears steamrolling frontline → recommendation-a-manager-approves, not auto-replenish; she is the gatekeeper of trust. Tom/Lisa: keep informed (stockouts hurt CX).
HITL checkpoints: (1) Every replenishment suggestion approved by the store manager before commit — evaluator: the store manager (knows what right looks like for their store); builds capability (managers stay fluent in reorder judgement while the system learns — the apprenticeship point) and consumes. (2) SKU-mapping / stock-on-hand sign-off before the model trains — evaluator: Priya’s data engineers + Raj Patel (the one person who knows StockTrak’s schema — key-person risk); builds. (3) Seasonal-buy forecast review — evaluator: buying team + Priya; mostly consumes (large, irreversible $).
The non-negotiable: no auto-commit to StockTrak — every suggestion is advisory, a store manager approves before write-back. It bounds blast radius on the broadest-scope initiative and protects a fragile, out-of-support system. Both Marcus’s note and Sarah’s policy say exactly this.




Sprint 3 — roadmap, risk & the go/no-go


Roadmap with gates: Phase 0 — Integration groundwork (~6 wks): StockTrak nightly replica + Shopify feed in staging; SKU-master cleanse for the homewares slice; cross-train Raj’s knowledge. Gate 0: trustworthy, reconciled homewares data + key-person risk mitigated before any modelling. Phase 1 — Pilot (homewares, ~3 mo): advisory daily suggestions ranked by stockout risk, manager approves. Gate 1 (go/no-go): forecast acceptance ≥ gate, pilot stockout ≤6%, no nil-stock regressions, nightly pipeline stable. Phase 2 — Scale: waves of categories/stores; move stable lines toward auto-suggest (still no auto-commit while StockTrak is EOL). Phase 3 (FY27+): POS replacement unlocks real-time + auto-commit.
Risk register (top): Right-in-aggregate / wrong-on-a-store (AI-specific: demo gap + non-determinism) — Med/High — mitigate: pilot, per-store validation, gate on worst store not network average. Integration under-scoped (AI-specific: data is discovered, not specified) — High/High — mitigate: $390K line, treat as data-quality program, Phase 0 first. Auto-write to EOL StockTrak — Med/High — mitigate: advisory-only, no auto-commit. Key-person risk (Raj) — Med/High — mitigate: cross-train, document, Gate 0. Manager distrust — Med/High — mitigate: manager-approves model + Sarah’s change management.
Assumptions register (load-bearing): (1) homewares SKU-cleanse delivers trustworthy stock-on-hand → shaky (no shared product key; “reconciliation is a project in itself”). (2) nightly batch / 24-hr lag is tolerable → holding (designed in). (3) StockTrak replica + Shopify feed stand up in ~6 wks → shaky (EOL, undocumented). (4) managers accept the suggestions (trust) → shaky (Sarah: they ignore what they don’t trust). (5) Raj stays available through Phase 0 → shaky. (6) StockTrak stays operational (EOL mid-2024) → shaky. (7) board’s prove-then-scale framing holds → holding (explicitly endorsed). Five of seven load-bearing assumptions sit on the data/integration layer — the most underestimated part of the budget.
Gate-1 call: PIVOT by default; SCALE only if all data-and-trust gates clear; KILL only if the core extraction assumption breaks. Because the load-bearing integration assumptions (#1, #3, #6) are shaky, gate-1 leans Pivot — hold scope at homewares and fix the data layer — even if the demo looks good, rather than Scale on a forecast built on stock-on-hand we can’t fully trust. Scale only if homewares data is clean, the pipeline is stable, Raj risk is mitigated, and managers are accepting suggestions with zero nil-stock regressions. Kill only if trustworthy data genuinely can’t be extracted from the EOL estate — at which point inventory waits for the FY27 POS replacement, it isn’t force-fit.
DDCD crisis line: a confident forecast under-orders the homewares-candle line for regional stores — turning the pilot’s flagship category into the exact stockout the board funded this to fix.





Fraud detection · $650K



Sprint 1 — scope it against reality


Readiness: data – Shopify Plus order data (value, device/IP, billing–shipping mismatch, tenure, payment method) is clean and adequate to feed a vendor, but we don’t have enough labelled fraud history to build our own (Priya’s reason buy wins). Legacy POS is poor, but in-store card-present is the smaller exposure – not blocking. organisation – the manual rule-based review is straining, inconsistent online vs store, and we cannot measure our own false-block rate today (the baseline we must beat). Sponsor is Marcus (CIO); Priya owns threshold/oversight design; Tom is consulted on anything customer-facing. build/buy/host – buy, don’t build: this is a vendor-integration + oversight project, not a data-science build. Fraud is an always-on control → it sits with Risk/Ops + vendor, NOT in Priya’s analytics backlog.
Scoped objective: cut card-not-present chargebacks ≥40% and fraudulent returns/refund abuse ≥30% within 12 months of go-live. In: online card-not-present detection via vendor + threshold tuning + human review of flagged-before-block. Human-checked (for now): the borderline/flagged review queue. Out: in-store card-present fraud (smaller exposure, poor data); building a proprietary model; any auto-decline without review.
Data (from Priya): data is “good enough for a vendor” – and that’s the point. The real data work isn’t ingestion, it’s instrumenting the false-positive rate so we can finally see it, and demanding the vendor’s FP rate at our threshold on data like ours, not a 99.2% headline benchmarked on other retailers.
Trust-tool split: clear-clean majority (low risk) → lean in / auto-approve at checkout (speed). Borderline/flagged (~8% middle) → PRECISE: human in the loop before any customer is blocked. The threshold is a business trade-off owned by us (Finance + CS in the room), never the vendor’s default.
“Good enough”: no single metric – a paired trade-off. Primary: chargeback reduction ≥40%. Constraint: false-block rate must be measurable and must not exceed today’s manual baseline; the vendor’s ~1-in-6-of-flagged default (≈1.3% of all online orders wrongly interfered with) is the number to tune DOWN. Unacceptable: blocking a legit customer (esp. long-standing/wholesale) without review – “a wrongly accused customer rarely returns.”




Sprint 2 — stakeholders & human-in-the-loop


Stakeholder plan: David (CFO, High/High) – owns the $3.2M FY25 loss (up from $2.4M) + the $650K; wants certainty on the 40%/30% cuts; fears runaway per-transaction fees ($0.11/order scales with growth) and a loss-cutter that blocks good customers (the board’s hard constraint) → tie spend to gates; show chargeback $ saved vs per-order cost; Finance sits in the threshold room. Priya (Data, Med/High) – owns buy-not-build + oversight design; needs her team NOT running it; fears a black box we can’t oversee → she sets the measurement spec + vendor-oversight framework, explicitly not the ongoing model. Tom (CS, Med/High) – his queue absorbs every wrongly-blocked customer (3+ escalations/month already, one furious wholesale customer); fears looking bad + the 4-hr SLA already slipping in sales → staff the review checkpoint with his experienced agents; position as augmentation; he signs off anything customer-facing. Marcus (CIO/sponsor) – tempted by the 4-week vendor go-live; gate him on the demo-to-production reality.
HITL checkpoints: Flagged-before-block (borderline) → experienced CS/risk reviewer checks and contacts the customer before any decline – “security verification” framing, never the word “fraud” (policy). Evaluator: senior reviewer (the evaluator’s advantage – they know real-fraud vs nervous-good-customer; a junior becomes a conduit who waves it through). Builds capability (deliberate judgement work grows tomorrow’s evaluators) and consumes. High-confidence-fraud auto-decline, if used → QA-sample + audit, human can override; mostly consumes. Threshold-setting reviews (Finance + CS + Priya) → human judgement owns the trade-off; builds institutional capability.
The non-negotiable: no customer is auto-declined on the vendor’s score alone – flagged transactions get human review (or a safe release-and-monitor path) before block. A wrongly blocked legit customer is the single biggest risk (revenue + reputation), the board’s explicit hard constraint, and the AI-principles “high-impact action = HITL” rule.




Sprint 3 — roadmap, risk & the go/no-go


Roadmap with gates: Phase 0 Baseline & diligence (wks 1–4): instrument today’s manual false-block rate (can’t beat what we can’t measure); vendor POC on RetailFlow’s own data; demand FP rate at our threshold. Gate: FP-on-our-data < now-measured baseline; contract clause locking threshold ownership to us (not the Enterprise-tier hostage). Phase 1 Pilot/shadow (online card-not-present only): vendor runs alongside manual rules; Finance+CS+Priya tune; flagged → human review before block. Gate (end-Q2 board review): chargebacks trending ≥40% cut AND false-block ≤ baseline AND review queue within SLA. Phase 2 Live online + extend to returns/refund-abuse. Gate: hold both at scale. Phase 3 in-store (out for now). Scale/Pivot/Kill → production.
Risk register (top): Demo-to-production gap (AI-specific: demo on THEIR data) – the 99.2%/62% are portfolio benchmarks on other retailers’ data, say little about our patterns – Med/High – mitigate: POC on our data, FP-at-our-threshold before signing. False-block at scale (AI-specific: non-determinism + trade-off + verification) – ~1-in-6 of ~8% flagged ≈ 1.3% of all online orders wrongly interfered with; reputational + lost sales; we can’t measure it yet – High/High – mitigate: human-before-block, instrument the metric, tune down. Black-box/lock-in (AI-specific: can’t verify why) – proprietary model, can’t explain a decline; bespoke thresholds cost extra – Med/High – mitigate: contract for threshold ownership + explainability + exit. Per-order cost creep (not AI-specific) – $0.11/order scales with growth – Med/Med – mitigate: fee cap, gate review.
Assumptions register (load-bearing): (1) vendor FP rate on RetailFlow data ≈ portfolio benchmark → shaky (benchmarked on others’ data); (2) today’s manual false-block rate is a beatable baseline → shaky/unknown (we don’t measure it yet – foundational); (3) CS has capacity + expertise to review every flagged-before-block within SLA → shaky (4-hr target already slips; reviewer notes inconsistent); (4) threshold config is ours, not vendor-locked → shaky (standard = vendor default; bespoke = Enterprise extra). Four-of-four shaky → why gate-1 is Pivot, not Scale.
Gate-1 call: PIVOT if the false-block rate on our data exceeds the now-measured baseline OR the review queue blows the SLA at end-Q2. Action: tune thresholds up (more conservative blocking), make human-review-before-block mandatory on all borderline (no auto-decline) until the vendor FP rate is demonstrably ≤ baseline on our data. Rationale: the board’s hard constraint is customer experience – a loss-cutter that blocks good customers “will not be judged a success”; assumption (2) is the core load-bearing one and it is unverifiable-or-broken at gate-1, but fixable by tuning + HITL → Pivot, not Kill. Scale only if (1)+(2)+(3) all hold.
Crisis example: the wholesale-customer blow-up (finance email) is the template – a high-value, long-standing customer wrongly blocked and furious; playbook = human-before-block + assume-good-faith “security verification” comms + a fast release path.










NoteThese are model answers, not the only defensible ones



A different group, reading the same RetailFlow documents and interviewing the same stakeholders, can arrive at a different — equally defensible — design. The point of the afternoon is the reasoning you bring to scope, checkpoints and the go/no-go, not matching a fixed key. Compare your group’s calls against these and ask: where would we defend a different call, and why?

title: Seven prompt techniques to try
text: These seven prompting techniques are reusable patterns for getting more out of AI on your own work — drafting a policy, weighing a decision, rehearsing a difficult conversation, pressure-testing a recommendation. Each one turns the AI from an answer machine into a sparring partner, a coach, or a panel of experts. They’re not about clever prompting for its own sake; they’re about keeping your judgement in charge of the output. Pick the one that matches the job in front of you, adapt the example prompt, and run it on something real.

title: Seven prompt techniques to try
text:
Where RTCF fits
RTCF — Role · Task · Context · Format — is how you structure any prompt, and it’s covered in the in-browser RTCF toolkit. These seven techniques are patterns you apply with it: they tell you what shape of conversation to run, while RTCF makes sure each prompt inside that conversation is well-formed. So you might use RTCF to set a single prompt up crisply, and use Role Play to decide you want a coached rehearsal rather than a one-shot answer. Think of RTCF as the grammar and these seven as the moves.

title: Seven prompt techniques to try
text:
Why these seven
These aren’t random AI tricks. They’re seven prompting patterns chosen because they build the judgement AI can’t supply on its own:

Reverse Prompting → comprehensive scoping and requirement gathering
Pros and Cons → analytical, evidence-based decision-making
Stepwise Chain of Thought → deliberate, defensible process
Role Play → communication and interpersonal skill under pressure
Debating → strategic and ethical reasoning across competing views
Practice and Self-Testing → low-stakes rehearsal with immediate feedback
The Expert Panel → multi-perspective analysis and synthesis

Each is easy to adapt to your own discipline. Let’s work through them with ready-to-use examples.

title: Seven prompt techniques to try
section: Technique 1: Reverse Prompting (for Scoping and Policy Design)
text:
Technique 1: Reverse Prompting (for Scoping and Policy Design)

What It Does
Instead of jumping straight to a solution, the AI asks you questions to help scope out all the requirements, considerations, and potential issues. This mirrors real professional work, where defining the problem properly is half the battle.


Why It Works Across Disciplines
Complex problem scoping is fundamental to every business discipline. Whether you’re designing a policy, a strategy, a system, or a solution, you have to think through multiple stakeholders, constraints, legal and ethical implications, and trade-offs. This technique forces you past the obvious surface issues and into the full complexity before you start solving.
Discipline-specific contexts: - HR: Policy and program design with stakeholder, legal, operational, and fairness considerations - Business/Marketing: Strategy development with market, competitive, financial, and capability considerations - Management: Organisational change with stakeholder, cultural, operational, and risk considerations - Supply Chain: Network design with cost, resilience, sustainability, and operational considerations - Accounting/Finance: Audit or valuation scope with compliance, materiality, and stakeholder considerations - Economics: Policy design with trade-offs, stakeholder impacts, and evidence considerations


Prompt (ready to use)
I need to draft a new "Flexible Work Arrangement" policy for a 500-employee
company that has both office-based and remote staff. I need to make sure I
cover all the key areas.

Your task: Ask me a series of yes/no or short-answer questions to help me
clarify all the requirements, considerations, and potential pitfalls for
this policy.

Ask one question at a time. Wait for my response before asking the next
question. Continue until you've helped me think through at least 10
different aspects of this policy.

Begin with your first question.


What Happens
The AI will start asking questions like: - “Will all roles be eligible for flexible arrangements, or will there be specific criteria?” - “Have you considered how this will affect team collaboration and meeting schedules?” - “What equipment or technology support will the company provide for remote workers?” - “How will you handle performance measurement for remote versus office staff?” - “Are there legal or insurance implications you need to address?”
You have to think through each question before responding. By the end, you’ve been guided to consider fairness, logistics, legal compliance, technology needs, and cultural impact — all before drafting a single policy sentence.


Variations for Different Disciplines

HRBusiness & MarketingManagementSupply Chain & LogisticsInformation SystemsAccounting & FinanceEconomics



“I need to design an onboarding program for graduate hires. Ask me questions to ensure I’ve thought through all the key elements.”
“I’m planning a diversity and inclusion initiative. Help me identify all the stakeholders and considerations through questioning.”
“I need to restructure our performance review process. What questions should I be asking myself to ensure it’s fair and effective?”




“I need to develop a market entry strategy for a new geographic region. Ask me questions to help me think through all the market, competitive, and operational considerations.”
“I’m launching a new product. Ask me questions to ensure I’ve scoped out the customer, competitive, and capability requirements.”
“I need to design a customer loyalty program. What questions should I be asking to ensure it’s viable and profitable?”




“I need to plan an organisational restructure. Ask me questions to help me think through all the stakeholder, cultural, and operational impacts.”
“I’m implementing a new management model. What questions should I ask myself about change management, training, and stakeholder readiness?”
“I need to design a change management strategy. Help me identify all the resistance points and engagement considerations.”




“I need to redesign our supply chain network. Ask me questions to help me think through cost, resilience, sustainability, and service considerations.”
“I’m evaluating a supplier consolidation strategy. What questions should I be asking about risk, quality, and financial implications?”
“I need to implement demand planning. Ask me questions about data, systems, and organisational capability requirements.”




“I need to plan a systems implementation. Ask me questions to help me think through stakeholder requirements, technical feasibility, and change management.”
“I’m designing an IT governance framework. What questions should I be asking about risk, compliance, and organisational adoption?”
“I need to scope a digital transformation initiative. Help me identify all the technical, organisational, and capability considerations.”




“I need to plan an audit approach for a complex client. Ask me questions to help me scope the audit scope, materiality, and key risks.”
“I’m designing a financial reporting control system. What questions should I be asking about stakeholders, compliance, and operational constraints?”
“I need to evaluate a valuation approach. Ask me questions about assumptions, stakeholders, and regulatory considerations.”




“I need to design a policy recommendation to address economic inequality. Ask me questions to help me think through trade-offs, stakeholders, and evidence.”
“I’m analysing an economic model. What questions should I ask about assumptions, limitations, and stakeholder impacts?”
“I need to scope an economic impact analysis. Help me identify all the affected parties and considerations.”






Try this
Use it as a pre-writing exercise. Run the AI questioning session, then draft your policy or plan. Now compare the draft to the questions the AI raised — did you actually address them, or slide past the hard ones?

title: Seven prompt techniques to try
section: Technique 2: Pros and Cons (for Decision Making)
text:
Technique 2: Pros and Cons (for Decision Making)

What It Does
The AI systematically analyses multiple approaches to a business problem, evaluating each option against specific criteria. This develops the ability to make justified recommendations rather than just stating preferences.


Why It Works Across Disciplines
Every business professional has to evaluate competing strategies and make balanced, evidence-based recommendations. This technique builds the muscle for weighing trade-offs, considering multiple stakeholder perspectives, and justifying strategic choices.


Prompt (ready to use)
What are the main models for conducting performance reviews in a hybrid
workforce? For each model you identify, provide:

1. A brief description of how it works
2. Three key advantages
3. Three key disadvantages

Evaluate each model specifically in terms of:
- Fairness and perceived equity
- Administrative overhead and manager workload
- Impact on employee morale and development

Conclude with a recommendation for which model would be most appropriate
for a mid-sized tech company (300 employees, 60% hybrid, 40% fully remote)
and justify your recommendation.


What Happens
The AI will identify 3-4 performance review models (e.g., annual reviews, continuous feedback, 360-degree feedback, OKR-based systems) and analyse each systematically. You receive a structured comparison that you then have to critically evaluate.


Push back on the result
Don’t simply accept the AI’s recommendation. Force yourself to: - Challenge one of the “pros” listed — is it really an advantage in every context? - Add a disadvantage the AI missed - Argue for a different option than the one the AI recommended, using evidence - Identify which psychological, economic, or organisational theory supports or contradicts each approach
That turns the AI output from an answer into a starting point for deeper analysis.


Variations for Different Disciplines

HRBusiness & MarketingManagementSupply Chain & LogisticsInformation SystemsAccounting & FinanceEconomics



“Compare different approaches to managing workplace conflict: mediation, formal grievance process, restorative justice. Evaluate each for speed, fairness, and relationship preservation.”
“Analyse three different redundancy selection methods. What are the legal risks and ethical considerations of each?”
“What are the pros and cons of using AI-powered resume screening tools versus traditional human review?”




“Compare different market entry strategies: direct investment, partnership, acquisition. Evaluate each for speed to market, cost, risk, and control.”
“Analyse different pricing strategies: penetration pricing, value-based pricing, competitive pricing. What are the trade-offs for market share, profitability, and customer perception?”
“What are the pros and cons of expanding through new products versus new markets?”




“Compare different organisational structures: functional, divisional, matrix, networked. Evaluate each for agility, accountability, and collaboration.”
“Analyse different change management approaches: top-down, participative, emergent. What are the trade-offs for speed, buy-in, and sustainability?”
“What are the pros and cons of different leadership models: directive, participative, delegative?”




“Compare different supply chain strategies: make-to-stock, make-to-order, mass customisation. Evaluate each for cost, responsiveness, and flexibility.”
“Analyse different supplier relationship models: single-source, multi-source, supplier partnerships. What are the trade-offs for cost, quality, and resilience?”
“What are the pros and cons of vertical integration versus outsourcing key functions?”




“Compare different systems implementation approaches: big bang, phased, modular. Evaluate each for speed, risk, and user adoption.”
“Analyse different IT governance models: centralized, decentralized, federated. What are the trade-offs for control, innovation, and efficiency?”
“What are the pros and cons of cloud-based versus on-premises systems?”




“Compare different valuation methods: discounted cash flow, comparable companies, precedent transactions. What are the pros and cons of each?”
“Analyse different audit approaches: statistical sampling, risk-based audit, controls-based audit. Evaluate each for efficiency, effectiveness, and client relationships.”
“What are the pros and cons of different capital structure options: debt, equity, retained earnings?”




“Compare different policy approaches to addressing unemployment: fiscal stimulus, labour market training, labour market deregulation. Evaluate each for effectiveness, cost, and trade-offs.”
“Analyse different economic models: Keynesian, neoclassical, behavioural. What assumptions and limitations does each have?”
“What are the pros and cons of different monetary policy tools: interest rates, quantitative easing, forward guidance?”






Try this
Use this to stress-test a recommendation before you take it upstairs. Put the AI’s comparison in front of you and argue each option’s case out loud — or with a colleague — using the AI’s framework plus your own reasoning. Where you can’t defend it, that’s the gap to close before you commit.

title: Seven prompt techniques to try
section: Technique 3: Stepwise Chain of Thought (for Process Management)
text:
Technique 3: Stepwise Chain of Thought (for Process Management)

What It Does
The AI walks through a complex business process one step at a time, pausing after each step until you signal you’re ready to continue. It slows down high-stakes processes that are easy to rush.


Why It Works Across Disciplines
Many professional processes are legally, ethically, or operationally sensitive. You need to understand why each step exists and what considerations apply at each stage. This technique forces deliberate, sequential thinking and real engagement with the process logic.


Prompt (ready to use)
I am learning how to properly conduct a disciplinary action process for an
employee with a pattern of repeated lateness.

Your task: Walk me through the entire disciplinary process, starting from
the very first informal conversation. For each step, tell me:

1. What action to take
2. What to document
3. What legal or procedural consideration is important at this stage

After you explain each step, STOP and wait for me to type "next" before
moving to the next step. Do not provide the entire process at once.

Begin with Step 1.


What Happens
The AI will provide the first step:

Step 1: Informal Verbal Warning
Action: Meet privately with the employee to discuss the lateness pattern. Use a calm, non-accusatory tone. Ask if there are any circumstances contributing to the issue (e.g., transport problems, personal issues, health concerns).
Documentation: Make a brief file note recording the date, what was discussed, and the employee’s response. This is informal, so no formal warning letter yet.
Legal/Procedural Consideration: At this stage, you’re gathering information and providing an opportunity for the employee to explain. Under principles of procedural fairness, you must give them a chance to be heard before escalating.
Type “next” when you’re ready for Step 2.

You have to actively engage with each step before progressing. It stops you skipping to “just fire them” without understanding the required process.


Variations for Different Disciplines

HRBusiness & MarketingManagementSupply Chain & LogisticsInformation SystemsAccounting & FinanceEconomics



“Walk me through conducting a workplace investigation into a bullying complaint. One step at a time.”
“Take me through the proper process for making an employee redundant in Australia. Pause after each step.”
“Show me the recruitment process from job analysis through to onboarding, step by step with legal checkpoints.”




“Walk me through developing a market entry strategy for a new region. Pause after each step so I can understand the rationale.”
“Take me through the customer journey mapping process step by step, explaining the key decisions at each stage.”
“Show me the new product development process from concept through launch, pausing to explain checkpoints and risk mitigation.”




“Walk me through conducting an organisational change initiative. Explain each step and the risk if we skip it.”
“Take me through creating a strategic plan from situational analysis through execution. Pause after each step.”
“Show me the merger integration process step by step, explaining what happens if we get the sequencing wrong.”




“Walk me through designing a demand planning process. Pause after each step to explain the dependencies.”
“Take me through establishing a supplier relationship from selection through strategic partnership. One step at a time.”
“Show me the logistics network optimisation process step by step, explaining decisions at each stage.”




“Walk me through a systems implementation project from requirements through go-live. Pause after each step.”
“Take me through the software development lifecycle step by step, explaining quality controls at each stage.”
“Show me the IT disaster recovery process step by step, explaining what could go wrong at each stage.”




“Walk me through the financial close process. Explain each step and why it matters.”
“Take me through an audit engagement from planning through audit opinion. Pause after each phase.”
“Show me the capital budgeting process step by step, from proposal through post-implementation review.”




“Walk me through conducting an economic impact analysis. Explain each step and the assumptions involved.”
“Take me through developing a policy recommendation. Pause after each step to explain the reasoning.”
“Show me how to construct an economic model from theory through validation. One step at a time.”






Try this
Use it to rehearse a high-stakes process you’re about to run for real — a disciplinary action, an investigation, a redundancy. Run it once, then ask the AI to introduce a complication at Step 3 (“what if the employee refuses to sign the written warning?”). Hit the awkward moments in the simulator, not in the meeting.

title: Seven prompt techniques to try
text:
Technique 4: Role Play (for Skill Building)

What It Does
The AI adopts a specific persona and runs a realistic conversation with you. Think of it as a flight simulator for the parts of your job where you can’t afford to get it wrong the first time.


Why It Works Across Disciplines
Business work is fundamentally about interaction — with clients, stakeholders, teams, and colleagues. You can memorise the theory, but until you’ve practised actually having a difficult conversation where the other person pushes back, gets emotional, or raises objections, you won’t develop genuine interpersonal competence.


Prompt (ready to use)
You are an experienced and empathetic HR Business Partner. I am a new,
nervous manager who needs to put one of my team members on a Performance
Improvement Plan (PIP).

Your goal is to coach me through preparing for and conducting this meeting
successfully.

Guide me through this step by step:
1. First, ask me why the employee is being put on a PIP
2. Wait for my answer
3. If my answer is vague or subjective (e.g., "bad attitude"), challenge me
   gently and ask me to be more specific and behavioural
4. Once I give you a solid answer, move to the next aspect: helping me
   draft clear, measurable objectives for the PIP
5. Continue coaching me through each element of the conversation I need to
   have with the employee

Throughout this process, if I say something that could be problematic
(legally risky, unfair, too harsh, or too vague), pause and give me
feedback on how to improve it.

Begin by asking me your first question.


What Happens
The AI becomes a coach who actively guides you through the preparation. If you say “I’m putting them on a PIP because they’re lazy,” the AI will respond:

“I understand you’re frustrated, but ‘lazy’ is subjective and not something we can measure or document defensibly. Let’s try again: what specific behaviours or performance gaps have you observed? For example, are they missing deadlines, producing work below the required standard, or not completing their assigned tasks?”

This iterative coaching helps you think and speak like a professional.


Advanced Variation: The Difficult Conversation
Once you’re comfortable, flip the scenario:
You are Sarah, a high-performing marketing manager who has just been told
you're being put on a Performance Improvement Plan. You are shocked,
defensive, and angry because you believe this is unfair and politically
motivated.

I am the HR representative who has to conduct this PIP meeting with you.

Your behaviour:
- Start the conversation by immediately challenging the fairness of the PIP
- Bring up a recent successful project you led
- Hint that you believe this is retaliation for raising a complaint about
  your manager last month
- Be emotional but not abusive. Show genuine distress

I will attempt to conduct this meeting professionally. Stay in character
until I manage to de-escalate the situation and establish a constructive
dialogue.

Begin the meeting. I will speak first.
Now you’re managing a realistic, emotionally charged scenario. You practise de-escalation, empathy, procedural fairness, and documentation — all while the AI responds dynamically to what you say.


Variations for Different Disciplines

HRBusiness & MarketingManagementSupply Chain & LogisticsInformation SystemsAccounting & FinanceEconomics



AI plays a union representative in an enterprise bargaining negotiation
AI plays a candidate in a behavioural interview who gives weak answers that need probing
AI plays a manager who resists implementing a diversity initiative
AI plays an employee making an informal discrimination complaint




AI plays a difficult customer objecting to pricing and demanding better terms
AI plays a competitor in a market negotiation
AI plays a skeptical internal stakeholder who needs to be convinced of a strategy
AI plays a partner company executive in a partnership negotiation




AI plays an employee resistant to organisational change
AI plays a union representative in a labour negotiation
AI plays a direct report who is underperforming and defensive
AI plays a peer manager with competing priorities




AI plays a demanding supplier or customer
AI plays an internal stakeholder questioning cost cuts
AI plays a logistics partner with implementation concerns
AI plays a regulator questioning compliance




AI plays a resistant end-user during system implementation
AI plays a business stakeholder with changing requirements
AI plays a project team member questioning a technical decision
AI plays a vendor in a software licensing negotiation




AI plays a client questioning an audit finding
AI plays a board member scrutinizing a financial decision
AI plays an internal stakeholder challenging a budget proposal
AI plays a regulator questioning financial reporting




AI plays a policymaker questioning an economic recommendation
AI plays a stakeholder affected by proposed policy
AI plays a peer economist with a competing analysis
AI plays a business leader concerned about economic impacts






Try this
After a role-play, scroll back through the transcript and ask yourself what you’d do differently — which techniques landed, where the conversation turned, what principle was guiding you. That reflection is where the reps turn into actual skill.

title: Seven prompt techniques to try
section: Technique 5: Debating (for Strategic and Ethical Analysis)
text:
Technique 5: Debating (for Strategic and Ethical Analysis)

What It Does
The AI examines multiple perspectives on a contentious issue, argues for and against different positions, and helps you see the complexity and trade-offs in a strategic decision.


Why It Works Across Disciplines
Modern business work lives in the grey areas where competing legitimate perspectives exist. Whether you’re addressing an HR challenge, a strategy question, an operational trade-off, a financial decision, or a policy call, you need to be able to argue multiple sides of an issue before making a defensible recommendation.


Variation A: Critique and Compare
I will provide you with two different "Bring Your Own Device (BYOD)"
policies from two fictional companies.

Your task:
1. Critique the strengths and weaknesses of each policy in terms of
   security, employee privacy, and clarity
2. Tell me which policy is stronger overall and why
3. Identify one element from the weaker policy that could improve the
   stronger one

Here is Policy A: [paste policy]
Here is Policy B: [paste policy]

Begin your analysis.
Use it when: you’ve got two draft policies on the desk (or you generate them with the AI, one deliberately flawed). Identify which is stronger and justify your reasoning before you commit. It’s a fast way to build critical evaluation muscle.


Variation B: Argue Both Sides
I want you to debate the pros and cons of using AI-powered tools to scan
resumes and filter job applicants.

Structure your response in three parts:

**Part 1: The Case FOR Using AI Resume Screening**
Make the strongest possible argument in favour of this technology. Consider
efficiency, consistency, potential to reduce human bias, cost savings, and
scalability.

**Part 2: The Case AGAINST Using AI Resume Screening**
Make the strongest possible argument against this technology. Consider
algorithmic bias, legal risk, lack of contextual understanding, potential
discrimination, and dehumanization of the recruitment process.

**Part 3: Critical Synthesis**
Critique both arguments you just made. What did each side get right? What
did each side oversimplify or ignore? Provide a balanced recommendation
with conditions (e.g., "Use AI screening only if X, Y, and Z safeguards
are in place").
What happens: The AI builds sophisticated arguments for both positions, then a nuanced synthesis. You can’t just pick a side — you have to understand the legitimate concerns and benefits on both.


Variation C: Multi-Stakeholder Debate
This is the most sophisticated version:
You will simulate a leadership debate on whether to implement a "mandatory
return to office" policy requiring all employees to work in-office four
days per week.

Create three distinct personas and have them debate this issue:

**Persona 1: The CFO (Chief Financial Officer)**
Focus on: Real estate costs, productivity metrics, and financial efficiency

**Persona 2: The CHRO (Chief HR Officer)**
Focus on: Employee morale, retention, recruitment competitiveness, and
diversity/equity/inclusion implications

**Persona 3: The COO (Chief Operating Officer)**
Focus on: Team collaboration, operational consistency, training
effectiveness, and company culture

Have each persona make their opening argument (3-4 sentences each). Then
have them respond to each other's points. Continue the debate through three
rounds until they need to make a recommendation to the CEO.

After the debate, summarise the key tension points and what a compromise
policy might look like.

Begin the debate.
What happens: The AI creates a realistic executive debate where each stakeholder has legitimate but competing priorities. You see that there’s rarely a perfect answer, only trade-offs and compromise.


Push it further
After reading the debate, push yourself to: 1. Identify which stakeholder made the strongest argument, and why 2. Propose a policy that addresses the COO’s concerns while minimising the CHRO’s risks 3. Explain what data or evidence would help resolve the CFO’s questions 4. Name the organisational or economic theory that best explains the tension between these perspectives


Try this
Run the two-sided debate before your next high-stakes recommendation — a board paper, an investment memo, client advice. If you can’t articulate the strongest case against your own position, you’re not ready to defend it.

title: Seven prompt techniques to try
section: Technique 6: Practice and Self-Testing (for Rehearsal and Confidence)
text:
Technique 6: Practice and Self-Testing (for Rehearsal and Confidence)

What It Does
The AI generates scenarios you can use to rehearse a judgement call, prep for a difficult conversation or negotiation, or self-check your understanding of a domain before a high-stakes decision. It gives you low-stakes repetition and immediate feedback — the reps you can’t safely get on the job.


Why It Works
Many professional judgement calls need practice you can’t get for real without risk: advising on a legal point, running a sensitive conversation, reading a tricky market signal. You need more reps than the day-to-day provides, and a colleague’s time to walk you through them is scarce. AI gives you a simulator — infinite scenarios, instant generation, immediate feedback, and difficulty that adapts to how you’re doing.


Use Case 1: Self-check your understanding of a domain
Prompt:
I need to advise on Australian employment law and I want to check my own
understanding before I do. I need to practice identifying which legislation
applies in different workplace scenarios.

Generate 10 short scenario descriptions (2-3 sentences each) where I need
to identify:
1. Which legislation is relevant (e.g., Fair Work Act, WHS Act,
   Anti-Discrimination Act)
2. What the employer's legal obligation is
3. What risk exists if the employer doesn't comply

After I answer each one, tell me if I'm correct and explain why.

Begin with Scenario 1.
What happens: The AI creates scenario after scenario. You test yourself, get immediate feedback, and keep going until you’re confident. Unlike a textbook with a handful of practice problems, this gives you unlimited reps.
Key feature: the AI adapts. If you get several wrong, ask it to simplify; if you’re nailing the basics, ask it to make them more complex.


Use Case 2: Application practice
Prompt:
I'm learning to identify different types of workplace conflict (task
conflict, relationship conflict, process conflict).

Create 8 brief workplace scenarios. For each one, I'll identify the type
of conflict and suggest an appropriate intervention approach.

After each scenario, provide feedback on my classification and intervention
suggestion. If I'm wrong, explain why and what I missed.

Make the scenarios progressively more complex. Start easy, end with
ambiguous situations where multiple interpretations are valid.

Begin with Scenario 1.
What happens: Progressive difficulty builds confidence and competence. You move from obvious cases to nuanced judgement calls — exactly the territory where a wrong read is costly.


Use Case 3: Rehearse a difficult conversation
Prompt:
I have to run a difficult salary negotiation with a key supplier next
week. I want to rehearse before I'm in the room.

Generate five progressively harder negotiation scenarios I might face:
each one a short situation (3-4 sentences) with a specific demand or
objection from the supplier. After each scenario:
1. Let me write my response
2. Give me feedback on what I did well and what to improve
3. Tell me what a skilled negotiator would do differently

Topics to cover: anchoring, trade-offs, breaking deadlocks, handling
"take it or leave it" pressure, protecting the relationship.

Begin with Scenario 1.
What happens: You walk into the real conversation having already handled the awkward moments. Each rep sharpens your phrasing and your fallbacks, so the first time you hear “take it or leave it” isn’t live.


Use Case 4: Close your own knowledge gaps
Prompt:
I need to make a recommendation on procedural fairness in a workplace
investigation, and I'm not confident I've got the legal requirements
straight. I think I might be confusing "being fair" with the specific
tests.

Create a 10-question diagnostic that tests whether I actually understand:
1. The right to be informed of allegations
2. The right to respond to allegations
3. The requirement for impartial decision-making
4. The requirement to consider the response before deciding
5. The right to representation/support

For each question, use a scenario. After each answer, give me feedback
explaining the principle — not just whether I'm right.

Generate the diagnostic.
What you get: A focused self-check that surfaces the edges of your understanding before you advise or decide — so the gap shows up here, in private, not in front of a stakeholder.


Try this
Run it solo before a big decision, or with a colleague: each of you generates five scenarios and tests the other, then compare answers and reasoning. The peer version catches the blind spots a solo run misses.


Rehearsal, not the real thing
This technique is for practice, not for the decision itself.
Why that distinction matters: - You can regenerate until you get scenarios you already know the answer to — so don’t let an easy run fool you into overconfidence - AI scenarios lack the rigour of a real, messy situation with real consequences - The point is to struggle and make mistakes here, safely, so you make fewer of them when it counts
The value: unlimited, personalised rehearsal that builds confidence and competence before the high-stakes moment.


Variation: Adaptive difficulty
Advanced prompt:
I want to practice identifying workplace bullying vs. performance management
vs. legitimate reasonable management action.

Create scenarios for me to classify. After each one:
- If I get it right, make the next scenario more nuanced and ambiguous
- If I get it wrong, make the next scenario clearer and more obvious
- Continue until I can correctly identify 5 difficult/ambiguous cases in a row

Track my progress and tell me when I've achieved mastery.

Begin.
This creates a personalised path — the AI adjusts the difficulty based on how you’re performing.


Self-directed progression
You can also drive the difficulty yourself through smart prompting:
Prompt:
I want to practice identifying workplace bullying vs. legitimate performance
management vs. reasonable management action.

Generate scenarios for me to classify. After I answer each one, tell me if
I'm correct.

Track my performance. When I get 8 out of 10 correct, automatically make
the next batch of scenarios more complex and ambiguous. When I get fewer
than 5 out of 10 correct, make the next batch clearer and more obvious.

Continue until I can correctly identify 5 difficult, ambiguous cases in a row.

Begin with Scenario 1 at medium difficulty.
What this achieves: - Difficulty that progresses with you - Mastery-based advancement - Confidence built through appropriate challenge - No technical setup — just good prompting


Why this matters
Traditional rehearsal is limited by finite scenarios, the cost of someone’s time to walk you through them, and no feedback until after the fact. AI removes those constraints — infinite scenarios, instant generation, immediate feedback, adaptive difficulty. The result: you rehearse until you’re confident, not until you run out of someone’s patience.

title: Seven prompt techniques to try
section: Technique 7: The Expert Panel (for Multi-Perspective Analysis)
text:
Technique 7: The Expert Panel (for Multi-Perspective Analysis)

What It Does
You consult multiple AI “experts”, each with a different professional perspective on the same problem, then synthesise their competing advice into a recommendation.


Why It Works
Professional problems rarely have a single “correct” answer. They sit at the intersection of psychology, law, business strategy, finance, and ethics. This technique forces you to consider legitimate but competing perspectives and make informed choices about priorities and trade-offs.


Prompt (ready to use)
I need to address high turnover in our sales department (35% annual turnover;
industry average is 18%). This is affecting team morale, increasing
recruitment costs, and impacting customer relationships.

Create three expert personas who will each analyse this problem from their
professional perspective:

**Expert 1: Organisational Psychologist**
Focus on: Employee motivation, job satisfaction, workplace culture, team
dynamics, psychological contracts

**Expert 2: Compensation & Benefits Specialist**
Focus on: Pay equity, market competitiveness, incentive structures, total
rewards, cost-benefit analysis

**Expert 3: Talent Management Strategist**
Focus on: Career development, succession planning, retention programs,
employee value proposition, organisational capability

Have each expert independently provide:
1. Their diagnosis of why turnover is high (from their perspective)
2. Their recommended intervention strategy
3. Potential risks or limitations of their recommended approach
4. What data they would need to validate their hypothesis

After all three experts have provided their analysis, I will synthesize
their recommendations and develop my own strategic recommendation.

Begin with Expert 1: Organisational Psychologist.


What Happens
The AI creates three distinct voices:
Organisational Psychologist: > “The 35% turnover suggests a breakdown in psychological contract or poor person-organisation fit. Sales roles are inherently stressful, but this level suggests systemic issues, possibly unrealistic targets, poor management relationships, or lack of autonomy. I recommend: (1) Conduct stay/exit interview analysis focusing on psychological factors, (2) Assess manager-employee relationship quality, (3) Review goal-setting processes…”
Compensation Specialist: > “At 35% turnover, you’re likely seeing pay inequity or uncompetitive base/commission structures. Sales talent is mobile and market-driven. I recommend: (1) Conduct compensation benchmarking against competitors, (2) Analyse pay dispersion within the team, (3) Review commission structures for fairness and achievability…”
Talent Management Strategist: > “High turnover in sales suggests limited growth opportunities or unclear career paths. Top performers leave when they plateau. I recommend: (1) Map career progression pathways, (2) Implement mentoring for high-potentials, (3) Create lateral development opportunities…”


Turn it into a decision
After receiving all three perspectives, push yourself to:

Compare and contrast:

Where do the experts agree?
Where do they conflict?
Which diagnosis seems most plausible given the evidence?

Synthesize:

“If I could only implement one intervention, which would have the most impact?”
“How could I combine elements from multiple recommendations?”
“What’s the minimum viable intervention that addresses multiple root causes?”

Justify with theory:

“Which theory best explains my chosen approach?” (e.g., Herzberg’s two-factor theory, social exchange theory, expectancy theory)
“What evidence would I need to collect to validate my choice?”

Acknowledge trade-offs:

“What am I deprioritising by choosing this approach?”
“What are the risks if my diagnosis is wrong?”




Variations for Different Topics
Variation 1: Policy Design
Three experts debate a proposed "Bring Your Own Device" policy:
- IT Security Officer (focus: data protection, cybersecurity)
- HR Manager (focus: employee experience, equity, privacy)
- CFO (focus: cost savings, business case, ROI)

Each argues for their priorities. Your job is to design a policy that
balances all three perspectives.
Variation 2: Ethical Dilemma
Three experts analyse whether to implement mandatory return-to-office:
- Employee Relations Specialist (focus: morale, retention, fairness)
- Operations Manager (focus: productivity, collaboration, efficiency)
- Legal Counsel (focus: contractual obligations, discrimination risk, WHS)

Your job is to recommend a policy that is ethical, legal, and operationally
sound.
Variation 3: Change Management
Three experts advise on implementing AI resume screening:
- Diversity & Inclusion Officer (focus: algorithmic bias, equity)
- Recruitment Lead (focus: efficiency, candidate experience)
- Risk & Compliance Manager (focus: legal compliance, audit trails)

Your job is to design an implementation plan that addresses all concerns.


Try this
After running an expert panel on a real decision, write yourself a one-page synthesis: where the experts agree, where they conflict, your recommendation, the theory and evidence behind it, and the risk if your read is wrong. That one page is what turns three opinions into a decision you can defend.


Why This Is Powerful
Most advice presents one “correct” approach. Professional practice demands navigating competing legitimate perspectives.
This technique teaches: - No single expert has the complete answer - Good business decisions balance multiple priorities and perspectives - Strategic thinking means choosing which perspective to prioritise when - Justification matters more than the specific choice - Real problems require synthesis, not just analysis


Combining with Other Techniques
The Expert Panel works brilliantly with:
Pros and Cons (Technique 2): > “After consulting the expert panel, use the Pros and Cons technique to evaluate each expert’s recommended intervention systematically.”
Debating (Technique 5): > “Have the three experts debate each other’s recommendations. Then you arbitrate and make the final decision.”
Role Play (Technique 4): > “After developing your strategy based on expert consultation, role-play presenting it to a skeptical stakeholder (CFO, union rep, CEO) and defend your choices.”

title: Seven prompt techniques to try
text:
Combining Techniques
The real power comes from combining these techniques. For example, a complete work sequence:

Use Reverse Prompting to scope out all the requirements for a new parental leave policy
Use Pros and Cons to evaluate three different policy models
Draft your chosen policy
Use Role Play to practise explaining the policy to a skeptical manager
Use Debating to analyse potential criticisms from different stakeholders

This sequence takes you through analysis, decision-making, communication, and critical reflection — a complete professional process.

title: Seven prompt techniques to try
text:
Your Action Step
Choose one technique from this guide and try it on something real. Pick the one that feels most immediately useful for a piece of work in front of you.
Copy one of the example prompts from your discipline’s tab, adapt it to your own content, and see what happens. Get comfortable with at least one of these before exploring the more complex applications.
These aren’t party tricks. They’re reusable patterns that keep your judgement in the conversation — and the AI just makes them available any time you need a rep.

title: Customer-service chatbot
text: Your group is the delivery lead for this funded initiative.

title: Customer-service chatbot
text:
The mandate
The board has approved $450K to deploy an AI customer-service chatbot across RetailFlow’s online channels. The case that won funding: customer satisfaction has fallen 78% to 68% and email response times sit at 26 hours. The chatbot is meant to deflect routine queries (order status, returns, sizing) and cut response times dramatically.
What the board thinks it bought: “a chatbot that answers customers, live in a quarter.”
Your job: deliver it without the predictable failures, and define what “good enough to go live” actually means.

title: Customer-service chatbot
section: Why this one is hard to deliver (not just buy)
text:
Why this one is hard to deliver (not just buy)

It is customer-facing, so a confident hallucination isn’t an internal embarrassment. It’s a wrong returns policy quoted to a real customer, in writing.
The data looks ready but isn’t uniform: returns and sizing rules vary by product line and have exceptions that live in people’s heads, not systems.
“It works in the demo” will be true within week two. That is the trap (Difference 2): the demo handles the easy 80%; the cost is in the angry-customer edge cases.

title: Customer-service chatbot
text:
The data reality (ask Priya)
Conversation/transcript data is good, the best of the four initiatives. Priya’s honest read: “good data foundation, ~6-month implementation, proceed with confidence.” But “good foundation” does not mean “knows your policies.” Grounding the bot in current, correct policy documents (RAG) is where the real work is.

title: Customer-service chatbot
section: Who to interview, and the tension they bring
text:
Who to interview, and the tension they bring

Tom Walsh (Customer Service Manager): his team gets automated first. The evaluator’s-advantage and apprenticeship-pipeline questions land hardest here. Who reviews the bot’s answers, and does that role build or consume his juniors’ judgement?
Priya Sharma (Data): feasibility and realistic “good enough” thresholds.
Emma Rodriguez (Managing Director): what was promised to the board, and by when.

title: Customer-service chatbot
text:
Your starting question for “good enough”
What deflection rate, and what maximum acceptable wrong-answer rate, makes this safe to put in front of customers? Where must a human stay in the loop before a response is sent?

title: Fraud detection
text: Your group is the delivery lead for this funded initiative.

title: Fraud detection
text:
The mandate
The board has approved $650K to deploy AI fraud detection across RetailFlow’s online payments, to cut chargebacks and fraudulent returns.
What the board thinks it bought: “fewer fraud losses, fast.”
Your job: deliver it without the predictable failures, and define what “good enough” detection looks like when both false positives and false negatives carry real cost.

title: Fraud detection
section: Why this one is hard to deliver
text:
Why this one is hard to deliver

The honest delivery question here is build vs. buy. Priya’s view: a third-party solution is more practical than building in-house. So your “delivery” may be a vendor integration and oversight project, not a data-science build. It is a different kind of plan, and a useful contrast for the room.
The two failure modes pull in opposite directions: too aggressive and you block legitimate customers (revenue + reputation hit); too loose and fraud slips through. There is no single “accurate”; it’s a tuned trade-off.
A vendor demo on their data tells you little about performance on RetailFlow’s fraud patterns (demo-to-production gap, vendor edition).

title: Fraud detection
text:
The data reality (ask Priya)
Priya’s honest read: “third-party AI more practical than building; our data-science team shouldn’t own this. Quick win with the right vendor, 3–6 months. Buy, don’t build.” Your roadmap should reflect a procurement-and-integration shape, with the threshold tuning as the real risk.

title: Fraud detection
section: Who to interview, and the tension they bring
text:
Who to interview, and the tension they bring

Priya Sharma (Data): argues buy-not-build; what your team should and shouldn’t own.
David Chen (CFO): owns the loss numbers and the ROI case; wants certainty.
Tom Walsh (Customer Service): his team handles the fallout when a legitimate customer is wrongly blocked. Who reviews flagged transactions, and how fast?

title: Fraud detection
text:
Your starting question for “good enough”
Where do you set the threshold, and who reviews flagged transactions before a customer is blocked? How do you measure success when the two errors (false block vs. missed fraud) have very different costs?

title: Your prompt library
text:
RTCF isn’t a trick – it’s being clear about what you actually want. Use this sheet to build a working set of prompts for tasks you genuinely do. Most prompts need all four only when the work matters; keep it lighter for quick tasks.



R – Role: who should the AI be? (e.g. “a sceptical senior reviewer”, “a plain-English editor”)
T – Task: what exactly should it do? (one clear verb)
C – Context: what does it need to know? (this is where your edge enters – the specifics only you hold)
F – Format: how should the output look? (length, structure, tone)







Tip



When does RTCF matter most? The scaffolding (Role, Task, Format) earns its keep on weaker or self-hosted models that need structure spelled out. A frontier model follows clear, natural instructions well – if you brief people well, you’re already most of the way there. The part that always matters is Context: the specifics only you hold. No model supplies that for you.





Write three prompts for real tasks from your role. Start with a naive version, then rebuild it with RTCF.


Task: ……………………………………………………………………
Naive: ……………………………………………………………………
RTCF: - R: …………………………………………………………………… - T: …………………………………………………………………… - C: …………………………………………………………………… - F: ……………………………………………………………………



Task: ……………………………………………………………………
Naive: ……………………………………………………………………
RTCF: - R: …………………………………………………………………… - T: …………………………………………………………………… - C: …………………………………………………………………… - F: ……………………………………………………………………



Task: ……………………………………………………………………
Naive: ……………………………………………………………………
RTCF: - R: …………………………………………………………………… - T: …………………………………………………………………… - C: …………………………………………………………………… - F: ……………………………………………………………………






Note



The two-pass lesson. Run your prompt once with thin context → slick, generic, anyone-could-have-it. Then layer in what only you hold (a customer, a contrarian bet, a taste call). Watch the output get a soul. That’s where your edge lives.

Your initiative: _______________________ Group: __________
You are the delivery lead. The money is committed. Before you can plan anything, you have to scope it against reality, and the only people who know the reality are down the hall. Interview Priya (Data) and Marcus (CIO) and use what they tell you.

6. Sprint close — defend your “good enough”
This is how you know the sprint is done. To the room, in one breath:

State it — the metric, the threshold, and the maximum error you’ll accept.
Name the evidence — where Marcus (speed) met Priya (data reality). What tension did you reconcile to land on this band, not a tighter or looser one?
Take one challenge. A group (or your facilitator) asks: “If the real error rate landed just outside your band, would you still ship — and why?” Your answer is the test of whether the band is defensible, not aspirational.

section: > If you can’t name the tension or flinch at the challenge, your scope isn’t finished — go back to section 5.
