4. Assumptions register
Every plan rests on assumptions. Name the ones your plan depends on, and how sensitive it is to each (see Delivery frameworks on the companion site). This is what makes the gate-1 call defensible.

title: Sprint 3: Roadmap, risk & the go/no-go
text:
5. The gate-1 decision
At your first evaluation gate, the data is ambiguous. Make the call and defend it with evidence:
SCALE / PIVOT / KILL (circle one), because: ______________________________________________

Killing a project that isn’t working, early, is success, not failure. The cost of wrongly scaling is far higher than the cost of wrongly killing.

title: Sprint 3: Roadmap, risk & the go/no-go
section: 6. Sprint close — the gate-1 board pitch
text:
6. Sprint close — the gate-1 board pitch
This is the sprint’s finish and the day’s capstone. You’re at the board with ambiguous pilot data. Three minutes, three parts:

Our call — Scale, Pivot, or Kill (one word).
The load-bearing assumption — the one holding/shaky/broken call from your register (section 4) that drove it. If Pivot, what do you narrow? If Kill, what did you stop early enough to be proud of?
Take one challenge. Another group (or your facilitator playing the board) asks the hardest question they can. Defend it with evidence, not confidence.

title: Sprint 3: Roadmap, risk & the go/no-go
section: > The point isn’t the “right” call — with ambiguous data there isn’t one. The point is that your call is traceable to a named assumption. A call you can’t trace is a call you can’t defend — and one you’ll wrongly scale.
text:
> The point isn’t the “right” call — with ambiguous data there isn’t one. The point is that your call is traceable to a named assumption. A call you can’t trace is a call you can’t defend — and one you’ll wrongly scale.
Leave Sprint 3 with: a roadmap with gates, a risk register, a documented crisis response, and a defended go/no-go decision. Together with Sprints 1–2, that’s your delivery design.

title: Three cognitive traps when working with AI
text:
Evaluating individual AI outputs is one thing. But there are three broader cognitive traps worth naming, because they shape how people relate to AI across everything they do with it. These aren’t technical problems. They’re human ones.


You read a news article about something you know well and spot the errors immediately – the reporter misunderstood the mechanism, conflated two concepts, or drew a conclusion the evidence doesn’t support. You notice because you have the expertise to notice.
Then you turn the page to an article about a subject you know nothing about, and you read it with full trust.
That’s the Gell-Mann Amnesia Effect: recognising unreliability in familiar territory, then forgetting that unreliability the moment you move to unfamiliar territory.
With AI, this trap is especially dangerous. You catch the AI’s mistakes in your own field, and rightly so. But the same AI, using the same process, will generate output on topics you don’t know well – and because the output is fluent, well-structured, and confident, you’ll be tempted to trust it in exactly the areas where you’re least equipped to check it.
This isn’t a flaw in the AI. It’s a flaw in how we respond to fluency. Polished language feels authoritative. The smoother the prose, the harder it is to remember that smoothness and accuracy are unrelated.
The defence: apply the same scepticism to AI output on unfamiliar topics that you’d apply to output on topics you know. If anything, be more sceptical – because you have fewer tools to detect errors.



The opposite trap is just as damaging: rejecting an argument, idea, or piece of work solely because AI was involved in producing it.
“That’s just ChatGPT” is not a critique. It’s a refusal to engage. The validity of an idea doesn’t depend on whether a human or a machine produced it. If someone presents a well-reasoned argument and it’s dismissed without engaging the reasoning, the dismissal is the intellectual failure – not the use of AI.
This is the genetic fallacy: judging a claim by its origin rather than its content. It shows up in workplaces where people dismiss a colleague’s analysis because they know AI was involved, and in public discourse where “AI-generated” has become shorthand for “not worth reading.”
The irony: people who dismiss AI-assisted work often accept ideas from books, consultants, and collaborators without demanding to know the exact cognitive process behind them. The objection isn’t about quality. It’s about the tool.



This one the AI actively participates in.
LLMs tend to agree with you. They praise your ideas, validate your assumptions, and avoid telling you things you might not want to hear. Researchers call this sycophancy – telling you what you want to hear rather than what you need to hear. It’s not a bug; it’s baked into how models are trained (optimised on human feedback, and humans rate agreeable responses more highly than challenging ones).
This matters for everything from writing to analysis to decisions. If the AI tells you your first draft is excellent, your argument is compelling, and your plan has no weaknesses, you stop iterating. You stop improving because the AI told you there’s nothing to improve.
The defence: prompt past it. Don’t ask “what do you think?” Ask “what are the three weakest points in this argument?” or “play devil’s advocate and tell me why this plan will fail.” Be direct: “Don’t flatter me. I need honest, critical feedback. Tell me what’s wrong before you tell me what’s right.”



These three traps – trusting AI more where you know less, dismissing AI output without engaging it, and accepting AI flattery as honest feedback – are not technical problems. They’re human ones. Awareness is the first defence.
Adapted from the AI Skills Passport’s “Three Cognitive Traps” guide.

title: Three cognitive traps when working with AI
text:
You read a news article about something you know well and spot the errors immediately – the reporter misunderstood the mechanism, conflated two concepts, or drew a conclusion the evidence doesn’t support. You notice because you have the expertise to notice.
Then you turn the page to an article about a subject you know nothing about, and you read it with full trust.
That’s the Gell-Mann Amnesia Effect: recognising unreliability in familiar territory, then forgetting that unreliability the moment you move to unfamiliar territory.

title: Delivery frameworks: readiness, assumptions & the go/no-go
text:
Three quick frameworks for the afternoon’s project – classify the ambition, check you’re ready, then make a go/no-go you can defend. Drawn from ISYS6020 (AI in Business: Strategy and Management) and Conversation, Not Delegation.


Classify the initiative before you plan. The quadrant sets the bar for readiness, risk and effort.









Process focus
Strategic focus




Incremental value
OPTIMISE – do existing things cheaper (e.g. automated data entry)
ENHANCE – better decisions, sharper competitive position (e.g. customer insights)


Transformational value
REVOLUTIONISE – reinvent how the work gets done (e.g. predictive maintenance)
TRANSFORM – new business model, AI-native offering



The further toward Transform, the higher the stakes – and the more readiness and human-in-the-loop you need.



Three lenses, run before you scope:

Data readiness – do we have the data, and is it good enough? (You discover this by working with it, not by asking.) Who owns it? What’s missing or messy?
Organisational readiness – who owns this initiative, who’s affected, who’ll resist? Is the sponsor still bought in?
Build vs buy vs host – how will we deliver it? Buy a vendor product, build it ourselves, or self-host? Each carries different cost, vendor lock-in and governance consequences.

Low readiness in any lens doesn’t kill the project – it reshapes the scope: start smaller, fix the data first, buy before you build.



Every plan rests on assumptions. Name them, and the Scale / Pivot / Kill call stops being a gut feel.
List the 3–5 load-bearing assumptions your plan depends on, and how sensitive the plan is to each:








Assumption (what must be true)
Sensitivity (if it’s wrong, the plan…)
Status at the gate




“the returns-policy docs are current and complete”
…fails on policy accuracy
holding / shaky / broken


“the bot will route to a human when unsure”
…erodes trust fast
holding / shaky / broken


“the CS team has capacity to review”
…checkpoints become a bottleneck
holding / shaky / broken



Then the call writes itself:

Scale – the load-bearing assumptions are holding.
Pivot – one key assumption is shaky but fixable (change the shape, keep the intent).
Kill – a core assumption has broken, and the cost of continuing outweighs the cost of stopping.

Killing early is success, not failure – the assumptions register is what lets you do it with evidence.

On the day: the Transformation Matrix + readiness check feed Sprint 1 (scope it against reality); the assumptions register feeds Sprint 3’s go/no-go. Both come from ISYS6020 and Conversation, Not Delegation – go there to dig deeper.

title: The trust tool
text:
The most useful thing to understand about today’s AI: it produces a convincing average. Fluent, well-shaped, usually about right – but “about right” and “exactly right” are not the same, and knowing which you need is the whole game.
The trust tool is two questions.



Average = “in the right ballpark” is fine. A first draft, a summary, a starting point you’ll shape further.
Precise = a single correct value, where close is the same as wrong. A contract figure, a dosage, a citation, an account balance.





Small = low stakes. Easy to reverse, cheap to get wrong, no one harmed.
Large = real consequences. Money, safety, reputation, someone’s job or wellbeing.













Small (low stakes)
Large (high stakes)




Average is fine
Lean in. Let AI run.
Lean in, with a glance. Sanity-check before it goes out.


Precision required
Use, then verify. Confirm the specifics.
Human in the loop. A person owns the decision.






This grid works at two levels – which is why it’s the spine of the whole day:

At the task (morning): “Do I trust this output for my work?” – average or precise, small or large?
At the project (afternoon): “Where does a human stay in the loop across this project?” – the same corners, applied to where verification and oversight are designed in.

The hardest question leaders ask about AI is “where does a human stay in the loop?” Most teams answer it by gut – everywhere (you lose speed) or nowhere (you lose trust). The trust tool makes it answerable. You stop arguing about AI in the abstract and start asking, task by task: average or precise, small or large?
Drawn from the companion book, Conversation, Not Delegation.
